new_findings.title: Replace yourself via ownership ladder, not task delegation

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

variants.claim: Scale past a plateau by training department heads to own problems end-to-end rather than escalating every decision to the owner.
variants.what_they_do: Five accountability levels: (1) wait to be told, (2) bring the problem, (3) bring problem + 1-2 solutions, (4) solve it and report, (5) solve it AND build an SOP so it never recurs. Most teams sit at 1-2, making the owner the bottleneck. Push C-suite heads to level 4-5; example: an acquisition manager independently bought aged warmed IG accounts and hired another VA to lift outreach capacity, then just reported it. Hold Mon-Fri morning team calls with only the department heads.

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

sources.quote: delegation actually works is if you delegate outcomes plus decision-making instead of delegating tasks
never bring me a problem without what happened... 2-3 solutions, your recommendation
there are five main levels of accountability... the first level being they just simply wait for the command on what to do.
what's different between level five and level four is they not only find the problem and solve it, but they also will build infrastructure to ensure that this problem never happens again.
making sure that you guys do morning team calls Monday to Friday.
if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin (§10 GAP): no org chart, ownership model, or decision framework; founder/tech concentrated on Josh and Evan.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ownership-ladder + decision-filter operating model so Palm functions (acquisition, content, chat, ops) each have an owner and decisions don't route through the founders — directly feeds the teammate-org initiative.

new_findings.topic: Creator vetting criteria and red-flag screening
new_findings.title: Screen creators for effort-mindset before signing; avoid purely passive-income-seekers

variants.claim: Agencies should qualify creators on mindset — specifically filtering out those who want purely passive income — because they disengage during the experimentation phase and waste agency resources.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe two acquisition filters: (1) prefer creators who have already tried OF themselves and seen some traction, because they understand the work involved and appreciate agency value more; (2) screen out creators who articulate 'I just want to make money passively' as their primary motivation, since they won't fulfill content obligations and the agency's marketing investment becomes a gamble. They also describe a 7-step creator vetting process for creators evaluating agencies (social proof → documented process → call → ask deliverable questions → watch for red flags like guaranteed numbers or pressure to sign quickly), which implicitly describes what a credible agency's pitch should contain.

variants.claim: Sign only creators who fit a defined ideal-client profile and screen out predictable red-flag situations rather than chasing anyone with a link in bio.
variants.what_they_do: Ideal client: already on OnlyFans, has/open to a niche, posts consistently, communicates, pays on time, has some IG/Twitter/TikTok/Reddit presence, and is coachable. Qualify briefly in DMs then on a call. Red flags that get declined or probed: under 3-4 months on OF, about to travel/move/have a baby, just had a breakup, churned through 5+ agencies recently, know-it-all who rejects all advice, asks zero questions, takes weeks to sign.

variants.claim: Operators sign more durable creators by qualifying them on the call against work-ethic and commitment non-negotiables instead of signing whoever replies.
variants.what_they_do: Opens the call human-first (camera on, 'how are you', hobbies) to break barriers, then qualifies: rejects short-term-money motives and 'overly involved' creators, requires committed daily hours and a fixed content/live cadence, and walks away if the vibe or non-negotiables fail.

sources.quote: me personally when it comes to new creators I definitely would say I'm kind of looking at the mindset that they're in before coming into this like if they're just in this I just want to make money passively then forget it it's not going to work
Here's my ideal client checklist. Number one, already on Only Fans... willingness to actually create content consistently... communication... payments... a bit coachable.
if they've gone through tons of agencies, especially in the last like 3 to 6 months... probably the common denominator is the creator.
work ethic trumps everything
have non-negotiables and have some respect for yourself
we prefer it's between 5 and six because then that allows a lot more content to be made

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is thin (§6) with no documented qualification criteria or creator scoring beyond the onboarding form fields. There is no mindset screen or preference framework for experienced-vs-new creators. The onboarding wizard captures logistics (name, commission, contract) but not fit signals. Palm's 75-convo market research was acquisition discovery, not an ongoing qualification system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a creator qualification layer before the onboarding form is sent: a 5-10 minute discovery call or async screening form with 3-4 questions targeting: (1) how long have they been on OF and what's their current monthly range, (2) what are they willing to commit to weekly in content production, (3) what's their primary goal (scale income vs. passive). Define a simple pass/fail criteria. Prioritize creators already making $1-5k/month who understand the platform — the speakers call this the ideal profile for fast results and agency appreciation.

new_findings.title: Google Drive onboarding folder: info sheet + logins + pre-made content scripts

variants.claim: A structured onboarding form (Google Forms) that captures age, location, device model, personal traits, and goals is the operational foundation before any work begins.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sends a Google Form at signing. Fields include: age, country, iPhone model (iPhone 13+ required for IG quality standard), personal traits, content style preferences, and creator goals. Login to OF account follows, then profile audit, promotions review, and a written growth plan.

variants.claim: A structured onboarding folder with three components — model info sheet, platform logins, and pre-made PPV content scripts — prevents miscommunication and enables the chatting team to start immediately.
variants.what_they_do: On signing, the agency shares a Google Drive folder. The model info sheet captures personal details (favorite color, food, pet names, etc.) that chatters need to maintain consistent persona in DMs. Logins for all managed platforms are collected at once. Pre-made scripts (photo/video sequences already shot) are also uploaded so chatters can sell PPV from day one. Expectations are set in writing: communication only via WhatsApp group, Monday–Friday 9–5 local time.

variants.claim: A documented model profile — covering chatting tone, physical stats, hobbies, and personality details — is essential so chatters can maintain consistent fan relationships without breaking immersion.
variants.what_they_do: For every creator they build a profile that includes: existing chatting style (especially for account transitions), height, age, bra size, feet size, astrological sign, hobbies, and other facts that come up naturally in DM conversations. Chatters reference this doc before and during conversations. Content is stored in a shared Dropbox/Google Drive the model can also upload to directly.

sources.quote: get up a Google Forms, fill it out — you want to know when, how old the model is, where the model's from, if the model's got an iPhone, what iPhone she's recording off
we always do iPhone 13 or above, they have to be, because Instagram care a lot about quality
within our Google Drive folder, we have three things. the model information sheets... the login... and then last but not least is the scripts
you need to understand their chatting style if especially if they're running their existing of account and you need to transition their chatting style to your Chatters
little things like their height age bra size feet size astrological sign hobbies things that just naturally get brought up during these conversations

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has an onboarding form wizard (name, email, commission %, state, contract, voice memo — §6) but no documented device requirement, content style intake, or goal-setting fields. The iPhone 13+ quality gate is not in Palm's onboarding.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's onboarding wizard to include: (1) device model field with a minimum spec gate (iPhone 13+ or Android equivalent), (2) content style/persona fields, (3) creator goals (revenue target, posting comfort level, explicit limits). Add an OF profile audit step as a formal onboarding milestone before first post.

new_findings.title: Inject scripted rapport breaks and push-pull frames throughout chat to create chase dynamic

variants.claim: Deliberately breaking rapport with playful negative statements (e.g., 'I was starting to like you until you said that') and push-pull phrasing prevents the sub from losing interest and causes them to actively seek the chatter's validation — which is the precondition for purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are drilled on two borrowed-from-pickup techniques: (1) Rapport breaks — any statement that actively discourages the deepening of connection, always softened as a joke (e.g., 'you and I would never get along because you're a math teacher and I have such trauma about math teachers'). These are not standalone — they must be followed up or contextualized so they don't read as mean over text. (2) Push-pulls — a positive statement immediately followed by a negative, or vice versa (e.g., 'you're actually kind of funny and I bet people teased you for it growing up'). These are to be peppered throughout the entire conversation, not saved for one moment. The psychological goal: sub chases chatter's validation, which increases desire to please — i.e., spend money.

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

sources.quote: a break in rapport anything that actively discourages the deepening or establishment of a positive connection
you should be doing push pulls you should really be doing breaks and rapport or specifically push pulls throughout the entire interaction
we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. This is a differentiating technique that separates high-performing chatters from low ones and is absent from most agency training.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include rapport breaks and push-pull scripting as a dedicated module in Palm's in-house chatter training. Provide 10–15 templated examples per technique that chatters memorize, then practice in role-play before going live. Monitor for correct usage in chat reviews — a chatter who never uses these is likely glazing, which suppresses sales.

new_findings.title: Install a system to capture proof continuously

variants.claim: Agencies should run a standing mechanism that captures every creator win, screenshot, and testimonial as it happens, since proof fuels both acquisition and trust.
variants.what_they_do: Routes every positive creator message, analytics screenshot, and voice note into a Drive/table immediately, so each win can later be turned into an ad or case study; recommends installing this even before running ads.

variants.claim: Systematically capturing and storing client wins (lead screenshots, view counts, clips) in a tagged database gives the CSM live ammunition to counter 'are you doing anything for me?' objections, improves retention at renewal, and provides case study material for new sales.
variants.what_they_do: Notion 'wins' database with fields: win type (leads/views/revenue/video performance), client relation, screenshot or media URL, date. Anyone on the team can add a win. KPI: minimum X wins per client per month set as a CSM metric. Wins roll up to client OS so the CSM can open a client record and instantly see a count and list of wins. Used on retention calls, renewals, and sales calls. Goal: ~20+ wins/month company-wide as a tracked number on the client success dashboard.

sources.quote: start installing the systems to capture proof like immediately
you need to like start having a system within your agency that captures proof
we want to create a culture at our company that we collect wins, we celebrate wins, and we stack wins. We have almost a thousand wins of just screenshots
I would try to get like at least a couple of wins a month for each client and make that like your KPI internally from like a client success perspective
when you're on a call with a client and they're like, 'Oh, I'm just not really seeing anything.' It's like, 'Dude, like we've gotten you 10 leads.'

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan/whale data (§9 PARTIAL) but has no documented system for capturing creator-facing wins/testimonials as marketing proof (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a lightweight proof-capture pipeline (Airtable table or Drive) that logs creator wins and testimonials, feeding both the acquisition funnel and a credibility library.

new_findings.topic: Role onboarding with daily winning formula checklist
new_findings.title: Give every new hire a 'daily winning formula' — a repeating Notion task with linked SOPs for each duty

variants.claim: A repeating daily task assigned to each team role, listing every responsibility with embedded Loom SOPs or email templates, eliminates guesswork, prevents the employee from inventing their own process, and lets the founder delegate without micromanaging.
variants.what_they_do: For each role (editor, CSM, appointment setter, etc.): create a Notion task template called '[Role] Daily Winning Formula' that repeats every weekday. Each checklist item links to an SOP (Loom video transcript pasted into Notion) or has a toggle with an exact email template. Example CSM items: 'Maintain daily updates in Notion by 10am', 'Review and QC all video content within 4 hours of submission', 'Send content to clients for approval', 'Update client portal'. Task auto-populates each morning. Pair with a 30-day onboarding course (gallery of Loom recordings) and pre-day-1 through day-6 onboarding checklist.

variants.claim: Replacing ad-hoc 'hire and hope' with a structured vetting process — Google Form application, defined training SOPs, and clear role expectations — results in hires who are productive from day one.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin's system uses Google Forms for initial applicant screening (filtering for baseline competence and fit before any call), paired with written training SOPs so hired VAs/chatters know exactly what to do from day one without hand-holding. Barry specifically credits a VA hired through this system as being immediately effective. The process is described as straightforward but most operators never implement it.

sources.quote: daily winning formula is basically what we'll do with this... I make it a repeating task every weekday... this is a task that just populates every single day for the client success manager
the reason your team would suck is because you're not actually equipping them with things like this to actually do well
every time I found myself doing something repeatedly, I basically just documented it, systematized it using Notion, and then trained someone else to handle it
before I was just hiring and hoping they were good but you showed me like a real vetting process like the proper training SOPs Google forms
the VA we hired through the system she knew exactly what she was doing from since day one

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Clerk roles (editor, ai_editor, chat_manager, creator) and an editor workflow with a day-counter task queue (§1, §10), but no documented daily winning formula, role-specific onboarding checklist, or SOP library linked to recurring tasks. The §10 gap notes no hiring criteria or scaling plan. This tactic is immediately applicable to Palm's editors and Amin (contractor posting role).
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's two most active roles — editor and Amin (manual posting) — create a daily Airtable or Notion checklist with 5–8 items each, linked to existing SOPs (or quick Loom recordings). Specifically for the editor: items like 'check task queue, pull next 2 slots, upload to Cloudflare, submit for approval.' For Amin: 'check Telegram topic, post per schedule, reply /posted.' This is a low-effort, high-leverage move to reduce dependency on Josh/Evan for day-to-day direction.

new_findings.title: Source creators from OF-video comment sections and push to a call

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

variants.claim: Find creators by searching niche keywords across multiple platforms, but treat satisfied-client referrals as the highest-quality channel.
variants.what_they_do: Search 'only fans' / niche terms (e.g. gamer girl) on Instagram, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok and filter for link-in-bio + recent activity. TikTok surfaces up-and-comers; Reddit is where whales and creators already are. Best channel = referrals: after a great first month in the green, ask the happy creator who else does OnlyFans.

variants.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
variants.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.

sources.quote: within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away
first one of course is Instagram... Your other option is going to be Twitter... The next one is going to be Reddit... You can also find them on Tik Tok.
by far the best is referrals... after you've delivered a great service... ask, do you know of anybody else who's also doing Only Fans.
you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is outbound-DM + referral but with no documented sourcing method, message template, or call-booking step (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a sourcing playbook (comment-section mining of help-seeking creators), a curiosity-led opener, multi-platform outreach, and call-booking as the CTA.

new_findings.title: Never link OF/Fanvue directly; warm accounts before linking

variants.claim: To avoid bans, operators never post adult-platform links directly on social and warm new accounts before adding any link-in-bio CTA.
variants.what_they_do: Always route via a neutral landing page (e.g. Get All My Links) since a reported direct OF/Fanvue link can trigger strikes/bans; spend the first ~30 days on profile setup, spaced posting and trust-score building before putting links in bio; avoid rapid-burst follows/comments and automation that trip platform limits.

variants.claim: New social accounts should be warmed as normal users and post content for about two weeks before adding the OnlyFans link to protect trust score.
variants.what_they_do: Acts as a normal user (scroll/like, follow niche on day 2, don't post day 1), waits ~2 weeks of posting before adding any link, and pre-builds accounts before signing a client so revenue never stalls.

variants.claim: New social accounts are warmed for about a week of human-like activity and niche engagement before any adult link is added, to build trust score and avoid early bans.
variants.what_they_do: Day-by-day: matching Gmail/name, scroll+like niche reels to train the algorithm, neutral (non-model) profile pic, follow ~15 niche creators/day, engage and like older US/UK 'simp' commenters under competitor posts to pull them over (never DM), then post first reel ~day 7 and only add the link.bio funnel 2-4 weeks in when followers justify the risk.

sources.quote: never post direct to Fan View. Always use the Get All My Links
warm up slowly, act like a human
post content for 2 weeks before adding a link
how you set up and warm up an Instagram properly within 7 days to avoid being banned
Build up your Instagram account slowly and patiently

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature with a 90-day cadence, Day-21 link handoff and Day-45 OF-CTA gate (§4), but real-creator warmup and link-safety rules are a gap (§4 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a real-creator account-warmup plus landing-page-only link policy mirroring the AI warmup gates (§4) to reduce strike/ban risk on creator socials.

new_findings.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
new_findings.title: Free teaser → $5–15 qualifier → $30–40+ escalation PPV ladder

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

variants.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more
the next video or picture will be a little more revealing you'll sell that for five to ten dollars and so on and so forth the next one from there will be 30 to 40
the first ppv messages should be pictures that are like five to ten dollars you're just trying to qualify if this guy is a spender or a Time waster

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

new_findings.title: Bootstrap Threads from existing IG account for follower carry-over

variants.claim: Creating a Threads account from an existing Instagram account gives an immediate follower boost that accelerates early growth.
variants.what_they_do: TDM connects each creator's existing IG account to Threads so that existing Instagram followers automatically carry over at account creation, providing an instant audience base rather than starting from zero.

variants.claim: High early posting frequency on Threads accelerates follower growth because the platform still incentivises new content volume.
variants.what_they_do: TDM posts 5–8 times per day per Threads account in the first weeks. Once the account hits 1,000 followers (or after a couple of weeks), they reduce cadence to 3–5 posts per day. They mix carousel posts (highest performers) with shorter comment/text posts as filler.

variants.claim: Threads algorithmically amplifies brand-new accounts' first posts, making it faster to gain initial traction than Twitter/X, where momentum takes much longer to build.
variants.what_they_do: Create a new Threads account, post immediately — the first post gets organic reach from Threads' push. Content is simple engaging text (e.g. 'somebody FaceTime me' style posts targeting lonely men). Bio links to Telegram rather than directly to Fanvue, then Telegram DMs are used to warm and convert. Positioned as a 'hidden gem' due to platform newness.

sources.quote: use an account that you already have on Instagram to make the threads account because this will give you a boost, a kickstart from all the followers that you have coming over
5 to eight posts per day per account. Now, after a couple of weeks or when you reach 1,000 followers, you can back off a little bit to three, maybe five posts a day
Threads is really underrated. It can bring in a lot of traffic especially right now because it's more of a newer platform
when you make a brand new Threads account or get a Threads account right off the bat, your first post is going to get views

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Threads strategy (§5 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). Palm does run IG accounts for creators (§1) but there is no recorded practice of extending those to Threads.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm sets up IG accounts for real creators, simultaneously create a linked Threads account to capture the follower carry-over benefit at no extra cost. Add Threads to the Phase 3 distribution rollout (batch-5).

new_findings.topic: Creator relationship management and milestone celebration
new_findings.title: Assign a dedicated model-facing manager to celebrate creator milestones and prevent churn

variants.claim: Proactively recognizing creator milestones (first viral video, top 1%, highest-earning month) via a dedicated relationship manager — not the CEO — is a primary driver of creator retention and prevents the agency-creator 'butting heads' dynamic.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe a specific failure mode: the agency founder misses milestone moments (e.g., creator hits first six figures) because they're buried in ops. The fix is a model-facing manager whose explicit job includes celebrating wins, providing comfortability, and making the creator feel involved in the agency. Examples given: 'you just hit the top 1%,' 'your highest month ever,' 'first 1M video.' This person also handles weekly or daily content feedback, direction relays, and expectation-setting — keeping creators informed and emotionally invested in the partnership.

variants.claim: Systematically capturing and storing client wins (lead screenshots, view counts, clips) in a tagged database gives the CSM live ammunition to counter 'are you doing anything for me?' objections, improves retention at renewal, and provides case study material for new sales.
variants.what_they_do: Notion 'wins' database with fields: win type (leads/views/revenue/video performance), client relation, screenshot or media URL, date. Anyone on the team can add a win. KPI: minimum X wins per client per month set as a CSM metric. Wins roll up to client OS so the CSM can open a client record and instantly see a count and list of wins. Used on retention calls, renewals, and sales calls. Goal: ~20+ wins/month company-wide as a tracked number on the client success dashboard.

sources.quote: highlighting those Milestones is big for sure like hey let's say you have a new Creator right you tell her hey we just hit the top 1% like congrats or like let's say her she just hit her highest month ever
we want to create a culture at our company that we collect wins, we celebrate wins, and we stack wins. We have almost a thousand wins of just screenshots
I would try to get like at least a couple of wins a month for each client and make that like your KPI internally from like a client success perspective
when you're on a call with a client and they're like, 'Oh, I'm just not really seeing anything.' It's like, 'Dude, like we've gotten you 10 leads.'

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator retention strategy or milestone-tracking cadence (§9 gap). Whale-hunting and Fan Tracker CRM exist for fan-side retention but not creator-side. Creator communication runs through Josh directly today (§10), which doesn't scale and leaves milestone moments to chance. No win-back or churn-signal framework exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As the model-manager role is built out (see delegation finding above), explicitly include milestone-celebration and retention cadence in the job spec: weekly check-in call or message, milestone alerts triggered from revenue dashboard (e.g., Airtable rule when monthly earnings hit new high), and a standard 'congrats' communication template. Add a creator-churn-risk signal to the Airtable Creators table (e.g., days since last communication, revenue trend direction) to surface at-risk creators proactively.

new_findings.topic: Fan connection before subscription as LTV driver
new_findings.title: Build parasocial fan connection via social persona BEFORE the OF subscribe to raise LTV

variants.claim: Fans who feel connected to a creator's persona — trusting her, finding her funny, being attracted to her personality — before they subscribe spend more and stay longer, measurably raising LTV 20–30% vs. content-first pages.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin's agency builds a distinct social persona for each creator so fans are 'invested before they even spend money.' The persona is expressed across IG/TikTok (top-of-funnel), and the brand promise is consistent with what fans find on OF. This is tracked: they observe LTV and CVR lift of 20–30% after implementing persona-first strategy.

variants.claim: Conversion on the paid platform is treated like buying any product: visible likes/reviews validate purchase, so operators manufacture social proof before driving traffic.
variants.what_they_do: Compares a profile with no likes to a one-star restaurant; boosts likes (e.g. creating throwaway Fanvue accounts via temp-mail to like wall posts) so the page looks established and trusted, alongside an optimized profile pic and multi-angle banner that show the model fully before subscribing.

sources.quote: if you can create that connection before the only fans like they're invested already before they even spend money that's what creates retention
I've seen my LTV go up like 20 30% along with CVR along with all these other metrics
Perform a like boost to give more social proofing
no likes equals poor conversion

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline (§1) is strong on viral content distribution but the baseline doc does not document a persona-consistency check between social identity and OF page experience. Fan retention strategy (§9) tracks whales and going-cold signals but no written retention playbook exists. The persona-to-LTV linkage is not captured.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a 'brand continuity' checkpoint: after the Creator Identity step (see content finding above), ensure the OF bio, welcome message, and pinned PPV messaging reflect the same persona defined in social. Document this in the onboarding SOP (§6). For §9 fan retention, add persona-fit as a variable when analyzing whale and churn data.

new_findings.topic: Sequential creator scaling vs. spreading across many low earners
new_findings.title: Max out one creator fully before onboarding the next; avoid managing many small creators simultaneously

variants.claim: Agencies stuck at $20K/mo with 8–9 small creators ($2–5K each) are less profitable and harder to scale than agencies with 3–4 creators each maximized sequentially; focus on one creator until capped, then move to the next.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin explicitly advises: sign one creator, put all focus on growing her to maximum revenue, then only take on the next creator when bandwidth permits. Barry runs 4 creators with one responsible for ~$50K alone. The agency avoids the trap of signing volume for its own sake. Paid promo layered on top of organic is used to stabilize revenue so the agency isn't 'one bad algorithm month away from losing everything.'

variants.claim: Reaching the first 10k/month comes from running the business as a real systemized operation—day-one onboarding, scripts, chatting, marketing all in place—and perfecting one creator before adding the next.
variants.what_they_do: Insists onboarding (Drive, content sheet/niche, scripts) be completed on day one not stretched over a week, gets Infloww scripts and smart messages set up before a chatter starts, focuses one marketing channel, and only scales to the next creator once one is fully systemized.

sources.quote: they have like nine creators all at like two three four five it's like just a bunch of creators
I get one model and I'll just try to max it out as much as possible and then from there when I feel ready and I have time enough to do it I'll just move on to the next one
It needs to all be done on day one
Perfect that for one model. Scale to the next

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's creator acquisition is Josh-DM-led (§6) with no documented sequential scaling model or creator revenue concentration strategy. There is no internal guideline on how many creators to manage at once or what revenue threshold triggers signing the next creator. This is a planning/founder-discipline gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's creator scaling model: set a minimum monthly revenue threshold per creator (e.g., $15K–$20K/mo stable for 60 days) before signing the next creator. Document this as part of the acquisition SOP (§6). Also evaluate current creator portfolio for revenue concentration — if one creator dominates, build a risk-mitigation plan (paid promo layer, brand depth) before pursuing new signings.

new_findings.title: Casino psychology: keep fans in the dopamine loop and stay in control

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: in a casino they do everything to make you blind to the fact you're there losing all your money
use fan notes to store this information
the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan intelligence via Fan Tracker (§9 PARTIAL) but has no chatting SOP using it (§8 OUTSOURCED); the rapport-and-psychology loop is undocumented build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Specify a rapport-and-control chatting playbook (onboarding DM, fan-note schema, no-price-talk, power-dynamic rules) for the in-house/AI chat roadmap, wired into Fan Tracker.

new_findings.topic: Tiered commission escalation to reduce signing friction
new_findings.title: Start at 20-30% commission and escalate by milestone to earn the full split

variants.claim: New agencies without proven results should offer a tiered commission structure — low percentage at signing, escalating to 50% only after hitting defined revenue milestones — to reduce creator risk and incentivize agency performance.
variants.what_they_do: One speaker describes his first deal: 30% upfront, moving to 40% at $15k/month, then 50/50 above $30k. The logic is explicit: 'I didn't know anything about this game' and going in at 50% without proof is unjustifiable for the creator. The escalator also creates aligned incentives — the agency is motivated to grow the account because their own take increases. A complementary tactic: for established creators already making (e.g.) $5k/month, guarantee that floor no matter what the agency does in month one, demonstrating good faith.

variants.claim: Deal structure should flex to the creator's revenue tier instead of applying a single 50/50 split across all creators.
variants.what_they_do: Uses tiered economics: beginners via a paid coaching/productized model (not 50/50), mid-tier (10-50k/mo) on 50/50 plus an upfront setup fee, and whales (100k+/mo) on a lower rev-share plus a fixed monthly retainer (e.g. 20k/mo retainer + 50% of upside above current revenue) so the agency is profitable from month one.

sources.quote: my first Creator I went in at 30% and I said at 30% I will start if we get past 15K it'll move to 40 and if I can get you past 30k we'll go 50/50 from there moving forward
you have to have tiers... Your deal structure has to flex to the tier
I'm going to charge you 20K a month retainer... I only want 50% of what we make you above that 100K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % at onboarding as a fixed field (§7), but there is no documented tiered/milestone-based commission model or floor-guarantee tactic for established creators. No internal baseline exists for how Palm's splits are structured or justified. This is a §7 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a documented commission framework with two tracks: (1) New creator / unproven account — start at 30-35%, escalate to 50% upon hitting defined monthly revenue threshold (suggest $10k/month, consistent with the 3-month target the speakers reference). (2) Established creator already earning — offer a monthly floor guarantee equal to their current earnings; charge full split only on revenue above that floor in month one. Codify both in the onboarding contract wizard.

new_findings.topic: Minimum viable team structure for one creator
new_findings.title: Three core hires outside chatting: account manager, SMM, and model-facing manager

variants.claim: An agency running even one creator needs three non-chatter roles: an account manager (OF vault/feed), a social media manager (IG/Reddit/Twitter), and a model-facing manager who relays communication between backend and creator.
variants.what_they_do: Account manager owns the OF side; social media manager owns all platform posting and content direction; a 'model manager' (ideally first-world, trusted) sits between both and the creator — giving her content lists, flagging trending sounds, celebrating milestones, and fielding her daily questions. This person acts as the Swiss-army-knife ops lead who reports to the founder daily on whether Reddit posts went out, tweets sent, IG up, etc. Founder only sets direction, not execution.

variants.claim: Free the owner by splitting the agency into core departments, each run by a manager the owner reports through, instead of doing everything personally.
variants.what_they_do: Typically three departments — client acquisition, marketing, chatting — each with a manager (e.g. chatter manager, marketing director, acquisition manager). The owner checks in with managers (not every IC) once each morning; managers run the ICs (outreach VAs, editors, chatters). Beginners can start with 3-6 chatters + one chatter manager. Trying to do it all solo makes scaling to $1M/mo 'physically impossible.'

sources.quote: account manager to just oversee the only fans account then you would have a social media manager to oversee everything that comes between all socials whether it be Instagram Tik Tok Reddit Twitter
you want to have three four main departments... Client acquisition, marketing, and chatting... have a manager in client acquisition, a manager in marketing, and a manager in chatting.
once in the morning, you check in with all of the managers.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org (§10) is thin: Josh runs acquisition/strategy, Evan runs tech/ops, Amin handles manual posting, editors handle video, and an external chat team covers DMs. There is no documented model-facing manager role, no SMM-layer employee, and no internal account manager layer. Communication between backend ops and creators runs through Josh/Evan directly, which doesn't scale. Palm's SMM consolidation (§11) is a tech fix; this is the human-layer equivalent that Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and hire (or designate internally) the three-role stack as Palm scales past 3-4 creators: (1) OF account manager per cluster of creators, (2) SMM coordinator owning platform execution, (3) model-facing manager as creator relationship layer. The model manager role in particular should be a priority hire before the next creator signing — it directly reduces founder bottleneck and improves creator retention (§9 gap).

new_findings.title: Daily feedback cadence to accelerate employee ramp 7x vs weekly

variants.claim: Giving employees feedback every single day instead of once a week makes them progress seven times faster, cutting ramp time from seven months to thirty days.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner or a designated lead delivers individual feedback to every employee daily — not a team standup, but specific corrective or reinforcing input tied to that person's KPIs and tasks. This is paired with formal performance reviews on a recurring schedule.

variants.claim: The fastest way to improve a new chatter is to sit on a live call with them while they chat, give feedback within seconds, and focus on fixing only one thing at a time.
variants.what_they_do: Manager joins a live call with the chatter while they work in real time. Feedback is immediate ('stop, do it again'). Manager identifies the single highest-leverage improvement point and works only on that until fixed, then moves to the next. Chatters are not 'saved' — manager tells them what to do but makes them execute it themselves so the learning sticks.

sources.quote: give your employees feedback once a week, I give them feedback every single day. That means my employees are going to progress seven times faster
Fast feedback loops. If you want someone to learn something quickly, you need there to be a short gap between when they do something and when they get feedback on it. And I mean seconds
Don't step in and do it for them. You can tell them what to do. That's how they actually learn

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented feedback cadence for editors, Amin, or any contractor (§10 — THIN). There are no performance review cycles or KPI dashboards per employee in the codebase. The closest analogue is the editor task queue in the portal, which surfaces output but not a feedback loop.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish a lightweight daily async check-in for each active role (editor, posting contractor, eventually chatters): a shared Telegram thread or Airtable comment thread where the reviewer flags one specific 'fix this' and one 'good job' per day per person. Formalise this before hiring the in-house chat team (~1 yr roadmap item, §8).

new_findings.title: Golden Ratio: 60 DMs/day for 30 days guarantees first client

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

variants.claim: Cold outreach signs creators predictably only when run as a system of high volume, long consistency, and value-first conversation rather than clever one-off scripts.
variants.what_they_do: Commits to 100 personalized DMs/day for 100 days (25/account across 4 accounts), value-first openers via voice/video note, and finding the avatar through conversation (10-30% qualify) rather than pre-filtering on the profile; built a 500k/mo agency on cold outreach before running a single ad.

sources.quote: For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day
the real unit of measurement is 100 personalized DMs a day for 100 days
voice and video cut through 10 times better than text

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no codified daily volume target or tracking cadence (§6). No 'golden ratio' equivalent exists internally — outreach volume is ad hoc.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum daily outreach quota (e.g., 60 DMs/day) for whoever owns acquisition at Palm. Build or adopt a simple lead tracker (Airtable view or spreadsheet) to log status and follow-ups, matching the systematic volume this competitor prescribes.

new_findings.title: AI-image watermarks (SynthID) are the new ban trigger; switch generators

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

sources.quote: Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception
there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI program is EU AI Act compliant with required AI label + watermark (§3), the opposite stance; for real creators Palm has no documented platform-risk/ban-avoidance policy (§4/§5 gaps).
palm_comparison.recommendation: AI line: note label/watermark compliance is in direct tension with the ban-evasion tactics gurus use, so plan for platform-compliant distribution. Real creators: adopt the human-looking-bio + safe link-provider hygiene now.

new_findings.title: Salary creator model: pay creator fixed rate, agency keeps ~90% of revenue

variants.claim: In a vertical-scale agency, paying creators a fixed monthly salary (e.g. $2,500–$10,000) regardless of page revenue allows the agency to retain ~90% of profits.
variants.what_they_do: Agency signs creators on a flat monthly salary tied to cost-of-living (e.g. $2,500–$10,000/mo depending on location). If the page earns $100K/mo, the agency keeps ~$90K. Creator has no performance incentive; agency owns all marketing decisions. This is contrasted with rev-share models where creator keeps 70%+ and agency takes ~30%.

variants.claim: Pairing a low-cost salary creator with AI volume yields outsized margins because output is decoupled from the creator's motivation or filming.
variants.what_they_do: Cites a student paying salary creators ~$1.8-2k/mo each, generating ~$15-20k/mo per creator via hybrid AI volume, keeping the bulk as profit since the salary model only needs to produce NSFW vault content.

sources.quote: I could theoretically be paying this creator $2500 every single month, depending on where she lives, upwards to $10,000 a month. I keep 90% of the profit.
he's paying 6K to make over $45,000

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses a commission/revenue-split model set per creator at onboarding (§7) — no documented salary-creator option. Palm's split rationale is not written down (§7 GAP). The salary model is a materially different deal structure Palm has not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'salary creator' tier to Palm's deal structure documentation (§7). Define eligibility criteria (e.g. creator has no existing following; Palm does all marketing) and salary bands by geography. This model makes most sense for Palm's AI personas or for raw-talent real creators with no existing audience, where Palm controls 100% of the funnel.

new_findings.title: Build backend capacity ahead of creator pipeline, not reactively after signing

variants.claim: An agency must have more operational infrastructure than its current client load requires at all times, so any inbound creator can be onboarded immediately without dropping balls on existing clients.
variants.what_they_do: The speaker frames this as a readiness posture: 'if I know that any given week there might be a creator that reaches out that I'm really interested in, I need to have more infrastructure than needed for my current client base.' Reactive hiring after signing = delayed onboarding, overwhelmed ops, damaged existing accounts, and lost creator trust. The prescription is to treat hiring/staffing as a proactive capacity investment, not a response to demand.

variants.claim: The most practical way to identify scaling bottlenecks is to ask 'what would break if our revenue doubled overnight' rather than asking 'how do we grow.'
variants.what_they_do: TDM frames the scaling question as a stress-test thought experiment: list every department and workflow, then identify which ones would snap under 2x load. The output is a prioritized list of systems, hires, and automations to build before growth demands them, not after.

sources.quote: you actually need to have a backend infrastructure ready to take on more profiles before those profiles even are coming to you
what would happen and what would break if revenue doubled tomorrow

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led and opportunistic (§6). There's no documented ops-readiness check before signing. The onboarding wizard captures creator info (§6) but does not gate signing on whether Palm has chatter/VA/posting capacity available. As Palm scales, this gap becomes a compounding risk — each new signing without backend capacity degrades existing creator performance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a pre-signing ops checklist to Palm's creator onboarding flow: before progressing a prospect to contract, confirm available chatter slots (external team capacity check), Publer/Amin posting bandwidth, and editor queue load. If capacity is at limit, flag for Josh to hire/expand before signing. This is a lightweight systems fix (§systems) that prevents the self-destruction loop the video describes.

new_findings.topic: Incremental hiring in step with revenue milestones
new_findings.title: Add team members and build systems in proportion to revenue growth — not all at once

variants.claim: Agencies that scale successfully add team members and formalize systems incrementally as revenue grows (every $10k step), rather than brute-forcing revenue and attempting to retrofit structure later.
variants.what_they_do: TDM describes a stepwise pattern: $10k → $20k adds first team members; $20k → $30k formalizes contracts; $30k → $40k introduces KPI tracking; $40k → $50k and onward continues compounding systems. The key is that structure is built in proportion to the revenue step, not deferred.

variants.claim: Hires should be added on a revenue-gated ladder — owner does everything to ~5k, VAs/chatters at 5-20k, managers at 20k+, ops director at 50-100k+.
variants.what_they_do: 0-5k owner solo; 5-20k first VA/chatter (after chatting yourself 2 weeks); 20-40k chat manager first then content/account managers; 50-100k+ ops director to execute proven systems so the owner exits day-to-day.

sources.quote: 10 turned into 20 but a couple team members came on board. 20 turned into 30 which meant that you started to build out proper contracts.
you need to hire managers

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's hiring and org structure are thin and undocumented (§10 GAP). There is no scaling roadmap that ties headcount or system builds to specific revenue milestones. Palm risks the 'brute force' trap the video warns against if growth outpaces internal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a simple revenue-milestone roadmap in Airtable or Notion: at each $X increment in monthly revenue, define the next hire, SOP, or system to be built. Even a rough version (e.g., at $X add a second editor; at $Y formalize creator KPI reporting; at $Z begin in-house chat hiring) provides the incremental discipline TDM describes.

new_findings.title: Prefer percentage deals with Western creators over LATAM salary models

variants.claim: Percentage splits with US/UK/Australian creators outperform paying monthly salaries to lower-cost-region creators because Western audiences spend more and the model carries no upfront cost.
variants.what_they_do: Moved off salaried Colombian/Argentinian models (high overhead, hard to reach US spenders, recurring raise pressure) to zero-upfront percentage deals (e.g. 50/50) with US/UK/AU creators whose audiences actually pay premium PPV prices.

variants.claim: Legitimate agencies take no money upfront and no hidden fees, charging only via the revenue split, which is itself a trust differentiator at signing.
variants.what_they_do: Takes nothing upfront and no hidden fees, contrasting against agencies that make creators 'buy their way in'; frames the relationship as partners not assets and lays all terms out from the beginning so they never change.

sources.quote: going from zero to 10K with a salary model is like a billion times harder than with a percentage model from the west
If it's a 50/50, it's pretty much based on what you make her. She takes half
nothing up front... I've been approached by a lot of other agencies and they all wanted me to buy my way into the agency
we really strive on being partners, you know, and not just treating you like an asset

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission per-creator at onboarding but has no written split/model rationale (§7 GAP); this benchmarks Palm's percentage approach against the salary alternative.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's percentage-over-salary rationale and Western-creator targeting as the codified pricing/commission position.

new_findings.topic: AI reel production — full synthetic content workflow
new_findings.title: Screenshot → GPT prompt → image gen → animation → CapCut caption: full AI reel pipeline

variants.claim: A fully AI-generated reel (no face swap, no real footage) avoids copyright and brand risk while still achieving viral reach when built from reverse-engineered prompts of high-performing inspo reels.
variants.what_they_do: Operator (1) identifies high-performing AI content on IG (targeting 100k–1M view reels, motion reels preferred), (2) screenshots the best frame, (3) feeds screenshot to Grok or ChatGPT to generate a detailed image prompt, (4) pastes prompt into Kling/MidJourney/CR to generate the image, (5) animates it in Kling, (6) adds captions in CapCut, (7) posts one reel/day for 10 days. At 10 posts they analyze which performed best and scale that template with a 'trial reel strategy' (20 reels/day).

variants.claim: The key to making an AI-animated reel go viral is identifying the specific physical action in the original viral reel and using that exact action as the animation prompt, rather than a generic description.
variants.what_they_do: When a viral reel is identified (e.g., 1M views), the operator distills the core action ('girl is doing a backflip') as the animation prompt fed into the AI tool (Kling). They then ask the AI tool to refine and make the prompt 'as realistic as possible.' The first frame of the recreated image is animated with this action prompt.

sources.quote: screenshot and ask Grok or ChatGPT to make a detailed prompt of this image and with the prompt you're going to paste it on Kling, MidJourney, or CR
post one reel a day for 10 days… you're going to analyze the data for 10 days… see which one performs the best
the secret prompt is what the original reel is doing to make it viral… if the girl is doing a backflip the secret prompt is going to be the girl is doing a backflip
ask the deep fake to refine the prompt and make it as much realistic as possible

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's §1 pipeline is strong on inspo-driven ideation for real creators but does not include a fully synthetic reel production track. Palm does make AI social content for some real creators (§0 note) and has AI persona infrastructure (§3), but a codified screenshot→GPT-prompt→image-gen→animate→post workflow is not documented. This is AI-persona-relevant but the prompt-reverse-engineering technique is transferable to real-creator content ideation too.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona line (§3, build-toward): document this 6-step synthetic reel workflow as a standing SOP. For real creators: evaluate whether the 'screenshot inspo frame → GPT detailed prompt → recreate visually' technique can improve brief quality in step 5 (Promote as filming idea) of the existing §1 pipeline. The 10-day test-then-scale cadence (post 10, pick winner, scale) is a low-cost experiment framework worth formalizing.

new_findings.title: Use Reddit profile bio as the single OF conversion point

variants.claim: 99% of NSFW subreddits prohibit OF links in posts but allow them in the Reddit profile bio, making profile visits the core conversion mechanic; where even bio links are banned, route traffic through Twitter or Instagram as an intermediate step.
variants.what_they_do: Standard setup: OF link in Reddit profile bio only (not in post body or comments). Post content drives profile clicks; profile bio converts to OF. Where subreddits ban OF bio links (e.g. after saturation): place IG or Twitter link in bio instead, grow those accounts as a secondary funnel, then convert to OF from there. This also unlocks access to additional subreddits that block OF links but permit social links. Reddit DMs used sparingly as a tertiary funnel (limited by Reddit ban risk on high-volume DM sending).

variants.claim: Reddit rewards posting volume of decent static/short-clip content across many subreddits, not the single viral hook-driven reel optimised for Instagram; ten 7/10 images outperform two 10/10 videos on Reddit.
variants.what_they_do: Agency repurposes a bank of ~100 photos per creator to sustain a full year of Reddit posting without new shoots. Videos used on Reddit are simple (10-15 sec, body-focused, no hook/narrative) and the same clip is reposted across 100 subreddits simultaneously. No time-of-day optimisation — just maximum post frequency spread across subreddits. Goal is aggregate impressions across subreddits, not a single viral post.

sources.quote: basically 99% of the subreddits allows you to post it on your profile on your Reddit profile in the bio. That's it.
instead of getting all these fans and getting to getting them to the only fans straight away… you could also say, okay, I want to funnel them to, for example, an Instagram account
give me a hundred pictures like like have a creator give me 100 pictures and I can promote her for the rest of the year with those 100 pictures
I would much rather have 10 pieces of content that are like seven out of 10, then I would have three pieces of content, two or three pieces of content that are 10 out of 10

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit funnel documented (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG accounts and has Publer scheduling (§5), so the IG intermediate-funnel variant aligns with existing infrastructure. The Twitter/X layer is absent from Palm's current stack.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When testing Reddit, default to OF link in bio as the primary CTA. Before launching a creator on Reddit, audit which target subreddits permit OF bio links vs. only social links, and set up the appropriate intermediate account (IG preferred, as Palm already operates IG). Track Reddit → IG → OF conversion separately from direct Reddit → OF to measure intermediate-funnel efficiency.

new_findings.title: Build a Telegram-sourced S4S network before signing creators to pre-load launch traffic

variants.claim: Developing a network of same-size trans creators and agencies for mutual shoutouts on OF, IG, Telegram, TikTok, and X before launch allows an agency to spike a new creator to tens of thousands of fans on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner joins as many OFM Telegram groups as possible, posts availability for S4S and GG collabs, and records all contacts in a spreadsheet. Matching logic: pages of roughly equal fan count do S4S (mutual shoutout); significantly larger pages are approached for paid GGs instead. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules promos from the entire network so that on launch day the new creator immediately receives a surge of fans. Once internal pages grow, new creator launches are boosted by the agency's own portfolio of existing creators ('internal boosting'), eliminating the need to pay for early GGs.

variants.claim: An agency entering a new niche should develop a promotional network of at least 10–20 creators and agencies before signing any creator, so that traffic infrastructure is ready on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Before signing a single trans creator, the agency owner spends time in Telegram groups building relationships with 10–20 trans creators and several agencies that manage trans creators. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules GGs and S4S deals with this network so that as soon as a creator is onboarded, a surge of guaranteed fans (e.g. 10,000 on day one via $5K in GGs) can be deployed immediately. This inverts the usual sequence of 'sign creator, then figure out traffic.'

sources.quote: you're going to gain most of your network through Telegram groups
when you onboard new creators, you pre-schedule promos from your entire network and all of your internal pages as well, boosting new pages to $100,000 in the first month
before you sign any trans girls, I'd say start developing your network — get at least like 10–20 trans creators that you're in contact with
as soon as you launch, have all of them pre-schedule everything so you have like $5,000 in guaranteed gains coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented S4S or network-building playbook (§5 GAP). The system is entirely absent from Palm's current ops baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Assign a part-time 'network getter' task (could be Josh or a VA) to join 5–10 OFM Telegram groups and begin logging trans-creator and agency contacts into an Airtable table (Name, Platform, Fan Count, Contact Method, Last Outreach, Deal Type). Even if Palm doesn't operate trans creators today, the mechanics of S4S and launch-day pre-scheduling are transferable to any niche and should be piloted on the next real-creator launch.

new_findings.title: Concrete performance guarantee reduces creator risk

variants.claim: A concrete, numeric performance guarantee (specific result in a specific window or you work for free) reduces perceived risk and differentiates from scam-wary creators' defaults.
variants.what_they_do: Uses guarantees like '2,000 paying subs in 30 days or we work for free' or 'clear buying activity in chats within 14 days or we fund your next shoot up to 1,000 dollars'; pairs the offer with one main angle (speed/risk/ease).

variants.claim: Standardizing the entire delivery system so the creator is the only variable is what makes an agency scalable, reducing dependence on any single creator.
variants.what_they_do: Runs every avatar-fit creator through one standardized machine (onboarding, evergreen content verticals, welcome message, funnel, chat monetization) auto-triggered on signing; same delivery took one creator 12k to 282k/mo and another to 44k/mo with no extra custom work, at 1.6M/mo and 60% margins. Onboarding is 'complete' only after the first full week of delivery.

sources.quote: if you don't get at least 2,000 paying subs in 30 days, we work for free until you do
choose one main angle... speed, risk, and ease
Productized fulfillment just means the same delivery model for every creator and we're allowing the creator to be the variable
one will go on to make 280k, one will go on to make 44k... and the creator is the variable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission model or risk-reversal offer (§7 THIN); creator risk concerns are the #1 issue in Palm's own research yet unaddressed in writing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a written risk-reversal/guarantee tied to a measurable early win, addressing the creator-trust gap surfaced in Palm's own research.

new_findings.title: Use carousels as growth drivers, text posts as volume filler on Threads

variants.claim: Carousel posts move the needle most on Threads, but text/comment posts are needed as cheap filler to hit daily volume targets.
variants.what_they_do: TDM designates carousel posts as their primary growth format ('goats') and text/comment posts as filler. Of the 5–8 daily posts, at least a couple are carousels; the rest are short text posts. They prioritise content that is shareable and/or controversial as the single biggest lever.

variants.claim: Shareability is the single highest-leverage variable on Threads — more important than posting time, hashtags, or any other tactic.
variants.what_they_do: TDM runs ongoing trial-and-error testing across content types and, once a shareable/controversial format is identified, they literally copy-paste the same post daily for weeks or months, observing linear compounding growth each time. They treat repeatability of a winning format as a scaling strategy.

variants.claim: Strategic comment baiting on Threads boosts engagement signals, but must look human and on-brand to avoid spam detection.
variants.what_they_do: TDM plants 1–10 comment baits per day per account (capped at 25–30 max) — posts or replies designed to provoke comments. They require the bait to be niche-relevant, human-sounding, and directly related to the original post to avoid spam flags. Volume is scaled based on page maturity.

sources.quote: comment posts as your fillers and then carousel posts as the goats. These are the kind of things that are really going to move the needle with your page
we've quite frankly just copy and pasted our posting every day for weeks, months at a time and seen similar linear compounding success every time we post the same thing
most important thing with comment baiting is that it looks on brand and looks human. It has to relate to the niche that the creator is in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is built around IG Reels and carousels but is not mapped to Threads formats. The inspo-driven pipeline could surface shareable content concepts that apply cross-platform, but there is no Threads-specific content classification.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend the content pipeline's format taxonomy to include 'Threads carousel' and 'Threads text post' types. When the AI vision analysis (Step 3) rates a reel high on shareability/controversy, flag it as a Threads carousel candidate as well.

new_findings.title: First DM: short curiosity-piquing message focused on a specific observable problem

variants.claim: The opening DM should be short, reference one specific problem visible on the creator's profile (e.g., wrong link-in-bio, no reels), and pique curiosity — never a long copy-paste paragraph.
variants.what_they_do: Agents identify a concrete, observable weakness on the creator's profile before messaging, then write a brief DM that names that problem implicitly or asks a curiosity-driving question. No feature-dumping or price-quoting in the first message. Outreach is sent from a personal IG account even with as few as 150 followers.

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

sources.quote: focus on the problem that specific model has. So this could be the lack of posting reels, the wrong link in bio
I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach message framework or script (§6 GAP). First-contact approach is entirely at Josh's discretion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a first-DM template library: short (1–3 lines), profile-specific observation + curiosity hook, no agency pitch. Test from a personal or agency IG account. This is a direct complement to Palm's existing pipeline — once a creator is signed the content engine (§1) kicks in immediately.

new_findings.title: Segment subscriber lists by content preference and renewal status to send targeted PPV messages

variants.claim: Sending PPV messages at minimum once daily, using a TV-advertising repetition mindset, is the single biggest lever for DM revenue most creators underuse.
variants.what_they_do: Creator sends PPV mass messages at least once per day, sometimes multiple times. Frames it explicitly as a repetition/awareness play (Tide detergent analogy): fans who don't buy message #1 may buy message #5. Explicitly rejects the 'one message per week' approach. Pairs this with multiple free posts per day on the OF feed to maintain presence.

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

sources.quote: you should be sending out pay-per-view messages at least once a day if you're not you are probably missing out on a lot of money

you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented PPV cadence SOP. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and the AI chatting project. The daily-minimum rule is a concrete, codifiable KPI.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a PPV send-frequency KPI to the external chat team's performance brief immediately: minimum 1 mass PPV per creator per day. When building the in-house chat SOP (roadmap), hard-code this as a daily task in the chatter checklist. For the AI chatting project, model automated PPV scheduling at 1× daily with A/B price variation.

new_findings.topic: Reel posting cadence — structured test window
new_findings.title: 10-day / 1-reel-per-day structured test before scaling posting frequency

variants.claim: Posting exactly one reel per day for 10 days gives a statistically meaningful sample (10 data points) to identify the winning format before committing to high-volume posting.
variants.what_they_do: New accounts post 1 reel/day for 10 days across all three content methods. After the test window, the operator reviews which reel format (face-swap, first-frame face-swap, or full AI) generated the most reach and then escalates to a 'trial reel strategy' of 20 reels/day in a specific order.

variants.claim: Copying an Instagram model whose recent reels have a views-to-followers ratio of at least 1:3 (views ≥ 3× followers) ensures you're piggybacking on a genuinely viral template rather than a stagnant one.
variants.what_they_do: Before cloning or face-swapping any source account, operator audits the last 7 reels and checks that total/average views exceed followers by a factor of 3. Accounts with views below follower count are skipped ('negative ratio'). This is the first filter before any content work begins.

sources.quote: post one reel a day for 10 days… you're going to have 10 posts on your feed and you just going to see which one performs the best
what you're looking for is identify someone that's actually pumping on Instagram so you can benefit from it… ratio of one to three

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's real-creator posting cadence is an acknowledged gap (§1, 🔲 GAP: 'real-creator posting cadence not codified'). This provides a concrete, low-overhead testing protocol. Palm's AI warmup plan (§4) has a day-by-day schedule but doesn't frame it as a format-test window leading to a scale trigger.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 10-day / 1-post-per-day structured test as the standard ramp cadence for new creator accounts (both real and AI). Define a 'scale trigger' metric (e.g., ≥1 reel hits 3× follower views within the 10 days) and document it in the account warmup playbook (§4). This closes the real-creator cadence gap and creates a data-driven go/no-go gate before scaling posting volume.

new_findings.title: Set expectations via call + concise video/docs emphasizing common beginner mistakes first

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

sources.quote: We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter onboarding materials exist at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'chatter starter kit': a ≤10-min Loom walkthrough + a 1-page doc listing top 5 beginner mistakes with example chat screenshots. Prioritize mistake-first framing over comprehensive rules.

new_findings.topic: Reddit asset spoofing to prevent cross-account detection
new_findings.title: Spoof every reused image via @spoofsybot; use RedGIFs re-upload to spoof videos

variants.claim: Reusing un-spoofed images or videos across Reddit accounts links them and triggers chain bans; spoofing tools strip the metadata/hash that Reddit uses to connect accounts.
variants.what_they_do: For images: run all reused assets through @spoofsybot on Telegram (paid, cheap, offers free trial) before posting. For videos/GIFs: upload fresh to RedGIFs each time — RedGIFs auto-generates a new file fingerprint, functionally spoofing the video. Never repost raw files across accounts.

sources.quote: spoofsybot on Telegram… you can spoof images there and you reuse content. Make sure you always do if you want to ever reuse content, you have to always spoof it
Red GFs technically does it for you because all you have to do in red gifs is just upload the video again and use a different link

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-persona account isolation system (§3 — hardware-isolated, separate SIMs, GrapheneOS) but no documented asset-spoofing workflow for Reddit or any platform. This is a direct gap when scaling multi-account Reddit traffic.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add asset-spoofing as a mandatory step in any Reddit posting SOP: integrate @spoofsybot into the content-prep checklist for images and enforce RedGIFs-only video uploads. Document this in the platform-risk runbook alongside existing device-isolation procedures.

new_findings.topic: Reel virality engineering via controversy and hidden props
new_findings.title: Embed subtle controversial or shocking background elements in Reels to drive comment engagement
