variants.claim: Adding a small unexpected or slightly explicit prop in the background of an otherwise standard Reel triggers curiosity comments that boost algorithmic reach, even without the foreground content being unusual.
variants.what_they_do: When replicating a standard trend (e.g. turn-and-twerk), place a mildly provocative item visibly in the background (example given: a pink dildo on the floor or wall). Viewers comment asking what the object is, generating engagement volume that the algorithm interprets as high interest. Agency frames this as 'adding your own twist' to existing trends to make content more controversial, funny, or anger-inducing.

sources.quote: anger and twisted trends really does help a lot… you can put in the background a dildo where when she bends over you can see it… people comment like what was that
adding your own twist to it to make it more controversial or funny or weird or make people mad… any type of engagement is good engagement

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) scores viral potential and instructs creators to recreate top-performing inspo reels. However, the pipeline does not appear to include a 'twist/controversy injection' step — it focuses on replication fidelity. This tactic adds an engagement-amplification layer on top of trend-replication.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'controversy twist' prompt to the content brief generated in §1's Promote step: when a filming idea is sent to a creator, include a suggested subtle background prop or scene element designed to provoke curiosity comments. This is low-cost to implement in the brief template and could meaningfully lift engagement rates.

new_findings.title: Vertical vs. horizontal scale: choose model before building ops

variants.claim: OFM agencies must consciously choose between vertical scale (few creators, salary model, high margin, high risk) and horizontal scale (many creators, rev-share, lower margin, lower risk) because the ops, team size, and marketing obligations are fundamentally different.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter maps two distinct agency architectures: vertical = 1–few salary creators, agency keeps ~90% of revenue, agency owns all marketing; horizontal = many rev-share creators who self-market with agency-provided SOPs, agency takes ~30% cut plus upsell fees. He recommends starting horizontal, then using that cash flow to fund a vertical page for higher margins.

sources.quote: There are two ways that you can scale an OFM company. Okay? You can either vertically scale.
I would take the income that I'm making from that operation and then start injecting it into vertical scale models on a vertical scale page and have the best of both worlds.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently operates as a hybrid leaning vertical — real creators with a revenue-split model, Palm owning social/content and outsourcing chat. Palm's commission structure is per-creator at onboarding (§7) but no documented strategic rationale for vertical vs. horizontal positioning. No explicit decision framework exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's explicit strategic choice between vertical and horizontal, or define a hybrid model. If Palm wants to scale to many creators, formalize the horizontal SOP-provision model (optimize what creators already have). If Palm stays vertical with fewer high-value creators, document the salary-vs-split decision criteria. At minimum, use this framework in founder strategy sessions.

new_findings.title: Pay micro-influencers flat rate or % to refer creators to the agency

variants.claim: Partnering with micro-influencers (10K–200K followers) as paid lead-getters via a Loom pitch and flat-rate or rev-share referral fee is an efficient bootstrap acquisition channel.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter identifies chatting agencies and micro-influencers with 10K–200K followers. He sends a personalized Loom video explaining the agency's value proposition with no extra work required from the influencer. Payment is either a flat rate per creator referred or a percentage of that creator's ongoing revenue. Once a creator is signed and gets results, she is also converted into a lead-getter who refers her friends, creating a compounding referral loop.

sources.quote: I would reach out to micro influencers between 10,000 and 200,000 followers. I would send them all a loom breakdown of my proposal.
After the exponential results, I would ask her to refer me to any friends that she has and offer her the same proposal as the initial lead getter.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outreach and referrals (§6) with no systematic referral incentive program or micro-influencer partnership channel documented. No Loom-based pitch process exists. This is a direct gap (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a formal lead-getter program: (1) identify 20–30 micro-influencers or OFM adjacent accounts; (2) create a 3–5 min Loom template covering Palm's value prop; (3) define referral compensation (flat $500–$1,000 per signed creator OR 5–10% of Palm's monthly take from that creator for 6 months); (4) after first successful creator, pitch her as a lead-getter with same terms. Document as an acquisition SOP in §6.

new_findings.title: Add DMCA takedowns and other services as monthly invoice line items to increase creator LTV

variants.claim: Adding recurring service upsells (e.g. DMCA takedowns at ~$2,500/mo per creator) to monthly invoices is an effective way to increase LTV without signing new creators.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter charges existing creators an additional flat monthly fee for DMCA takedown services on top of the standard commission split. At 16 creators × $2,500 = $40,000/mo in additional recurring revenue layered on top of the rev-share income. He frames this as a high-LTV, low-churn upsell.

sources.quote: Each one of these creators, I can charge an un on top fee of, let's say, an extra $2,500 a month for DMCA takedowns, increasing the LTV up here.
I would then start adding cost of new services to the monthly invoices such as DMCA takedowns to start increasing the LTB of my creators.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's invoicing system auto-computes commission % per creator (§7) but no upsell service tiers or add-on line items are documented. LTV expansion beyond the base split is a §7 GAP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a Palm services menu with priced add-ons: DMCA monitoring/takedowns, enhanced analytics reporting, paid traffic management, etc. Add these as optional line items in the invoice system (§7). Start by piloting DMCA takedown service (partner with an existing vendor) and offer to all current creators at a flat monthly fee. This requires no new creator acquisition to increase revenue.

new_findings.title: Hire one operations person per 10–20 creators to handle client comms and daily duties

variants.claim: A practical delegation trigger for a horizontal agency is one account manager hired for every 10–20 creators, focused purely on client communication and routine tasks.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter's explicit hiring cadence: start solo, outsource chatting/content to a chatting agency immediately, hire first internal person when workload is unmanageable (focused on creator comms and daily duties), then scale at 1 hire per 10–20 creators. At 16 creators with in-house chatting, he calculates ~48 chatters (3 per creator) plus 4+ managers for oversight pods of 10.

sources.quote: Once I surpass the 10 model mark, I'm going to start hiring one person for every 10 models to keep up with the client communication and all the duties that they need to take on.
You need about 48 people that you need to manage. Of those 48 people, you're going to need pods of 10 with management.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is currently Josh + Evan + Amin + editors + external chat team (§10) with no documented hiring trigger, org scaling plan, or headcount ratios. §10 GAP on org chart and scaling plan.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's hiring trigger thresholds: (1) at what creator count does Palm hire a dedicated client-success/account-manager role; (2) document the chatter-to-creator ratio Palm's external team operates at; (3) use this as input for the in-house chat team roadmap (§8). A 1:10–20 creator-to-account-manager ratio is a concrete benchmark to adopt.

new_findings.topic: Virality pattern recognition as a learnable skill
new_findings.title: Train agency operators to diagnose WHY specific video moments drive views/comments

variants.claim: Learning to identify the precise moment in a video responsible for views or comments — not just copying formats — is the analytical skill that scales organic revenue past $10K/mo.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin reviews videos with operators on calls and annotates specific timestamps: 'this point in the video is why it got views; this point is why it got comments.' Over 4–6 months of repeated exposure, operators internalize the pattern and can evaluate new content without needing external input. It is described as time + experience compressed through deliberate coaching.

sources.quote: every single one of our calls I would try and underline exactly what made a video go viral like why some videos worked why some didn't
you can look at a video and be like this point in the video is why I got so many views or this point in the video is why I got so many comments

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) uses AI vision analysis to score viral potential (step 3: transcript, visual breakdown, hook, viral score). This is automated signal — but the human analytical skill of diagnosing WHY a specific moment works is not documented as a training output for Palm staff. Palm has the data layer; the interpretive training layer is a gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'virality annotation' training protocol for Palm editors and content managers: when reviewing top-performing reels, require annotators to tag the exact frame/moment that drove the metric and the mechanism (hook, pattern interrupt, comment bait, etc.). Accumulate these as a labeled training set both for future AI scoring refinement and for human operator skill-building.

new_findings.topic: AI face-swap content — copyright and DMCA exposure
new_findings.title: Full face-swap of real creator content carries DMCA/ban risk; first-frame swap mitigates it

variants.claim: Directly cloning and face-swapping a real creator's existing reel is de facto content theft and exposes the account to DMCA takedowns and being 'burned' by the original model; animating only from a first frame or generating fully synthetic content avoids this.
variants.what_they_do: Operator explicitly calls out Method 1 (full video face-swap) as carrying 'brand risk' and DMCA risk because the underlying video belongs to the source creator who can issue a takedown. Method 2 (first-frame face-swap + AI animation) and Method 3 (fully synthetic) are positioned as the safer alternatives because no original footage is reproduced — only a single still frame is used as reference or content is 100% generated.

sources.quote: the only problem with this method is that it's sort of stealing content so the model can actually make you burn because you're just stealing her content and face swapping it
it's authentic, you don't have copyright issue and there's no brand risk

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI persona system (§3) includes EU AI Act compliance (AI label + watermark) and hardware isolation, but does not document a content-sourcing risk framework distinguishing safe vs. risky inspo usage. Palm already makes AI social content (§0), so this risk applies now.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a content-sourcing risk policy to the AI persona SOP (§3): explicitly prohibit full-video face-swaps of identifiable real creators' content; permit first-frame reference or fully synthetic generation only. Document this as a platform-risk control alongside existing EU AI Act compliance measures. Flag in editor/operator training.

new_findings.title: Ad creative is a volume game (1-5 ads per 1k spend)

variants.claim: Acquisition creative is a volume game where consistent high-output testing of hooks, not single perfect ads, finds the winners.
variants.what_they_do: Targets 1-5 ads per 1k monthly spend, 5-8 new ads/week, with roughly 50 hooks to 5 ad bodies to 2 CTAs; winners are then 'kaleidoscoped' (swap actor, background, speed, captions). Most agencies under-advertise and quit after one failed attempt.

sources.quote: you ideally want to be aiming for one to five ads per 1k in monthly spend
if you're going to be posting like five to 10 ads a week, like you need like 50 hooks

palm_comparison.vs_us: No paid acquisition program at Palm (§6). Relevant later, but the volume-testing principle also maps to Palm's social content engine (§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm runs paid acquisition, commit to a fixed weekly ad/hook output cadence and a hook-testing ratio rather than chasing one perfect ad; reuse the inspo pipeline's volume discipline.

new_findings.title: Automate fan re-engagement so chatters focus only on inbound

variants.claim: Revenue scales when automated, rotating 'smart message' sequences trigger on fan activity to remove human error and free chatters to work only inbound conversations.
variants.what_they_do: Sets up Infloww smart-message 'nudge' collections that fire when a fan comes online (after a delay), rotates ~10 collections every two weeks so messages feel fresh, auto-unsends ignored sequences, and stops the sequence once the fan replies—so chatters never do manual outreach.

sources.quote: you want to completely remove human error from chatters
this feature has made me hundreds of thousands of dollars for all my models

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's tech spine has Publer scheduling but no fan-CRM-driven re-engagement automation tied to chatting (§8/§9 PARTIAL).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Plan a smart-message-style automated re-engagement layer (activity-triggered, rotating, auto-unsend) for Palm's future fan CRM / AI-chat stack.

new_findings.title: Spin up backup/secondary accounts and post across many platforms

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution strategy (Reddit, multi-account, S4S) is an explicit GAP (§5); cadence is partially uncodified (§1). Note: this overlaps Palm's AI warmup multi-account capability (§3/§4).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-account/multi-platform traffic strategy with tracking links for attribution and a week-ahead scheduling cadence; reuse AI-warmup account infrastructure.

new_findings.title: Hire chatters on flat rate, commission, or hybrid — reinvest first revenue

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

sources.quote: you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) so this loop is bypassed at the current stage. However, Palm has no documented chatter comp model, hiring criteria, or onboarding process for when it builds an in-house team. This is a build-toward gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin documenting chatter comp benchmarks now (flat rate ranges, commission % norms, hybrid structures) sourced from competitor content like this. Build a chatter job description and scoring rubric ahead of the in-house chat team launch. This prevents scrambling when the timeline moves up.

new_findings.title: Use the onboarding process itself as a passive screening tool for creator quality and reliability

variants.claim: How a model responds during onboarding — responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback, implementation of early requests — is a leading indicator of her long-term revenue potential and whether the agency should continue investing in her.
variants.what_they_do: They observe model behavior during the onboarding phase as a quality signal: slow responses, failure to implement feedback, or resistance to criticism are treated as early warnings that the creator will not reach top-tier earnings. They acknowledge that new agencies must work with whoever they can get but advise building judgment over time to screen for quality clients.

sources.quote: if they're flaky or they don't respond very good or they take forever to respond or they don't Implement criticism you most likely don't have a very high quality client

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (§6) — it captures data but generates no behavioral signal about the creator. There is no documented creator-quality scoring or early-stage filtering criteria.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 30-day post-onboarding creator quality review field to the Creators Airtable table. Score creators on: (1) content submission timeliness, (2) responsiveness on Telegram, (3) implementation of feedback from first weekly call. Use scores to prioritize agency resource allocation and, as the roster scales, to inform future signing decisions.

new_findings.title: Length-based PPV pricing ladder: $5–$8 short, $8–$20 long, nothing under 1 min

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
variants.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.

sources.quote: if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 — #1 gap flagged). No internal baseline exists for price ladders, anchoring, or length-based rules. This is pure learn-from-others territory.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a default PPV pricing framework for all Palm creators: <1 min = free/engagement only; 1–3 min = $5–$8; 3–8 min = $8–$15; 8+ min = $15–$25. Document as a pricing SOP and share with the external chat team so they apply consistent pricing in DMs. Review per-creator after 30 days and adjust based on conversion data already in the system.

new_findings.topic: Reddit subreddit and content testing cadence
new_findings.title: Test subreddits by upvote threshold; swap non-performers; prioritise content quality above all

variants.claim: Content quality is the single most important Reddit variable — good content can overcome mediocre subreddits, but bad content fails even in perfect subreddits.
variants.what_they_do: Start with a niche (e.g. feet), find subreddits by searching and crawling competitor model pages. Post in each; if a subreddit doesn't reach ~20+ upvotes, swap it for a new one. Simultaneously test content by finding top-performing posts within target subreddits and asking the creator to recreate them with a personal twist. Scale to 20 subreddits/account then plateau for a month before expanding further.

sources.quote: we basically test the subreddit… if it doesn't get like 20 plus uploads a day then we just change them out for new ones

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content ideation engine (§1) is strong for social reels but is not applied to Reddit content selection. No subreddit testing framework exists (§5 🔲 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adapt Palm's existing inspo-scrape pipeline (§1) to pull top Reddit posts from target subreddits as content briefs — same viral-score logic, different surface. Add subreddit performance tracking (upvote rate per post) to Airtable so low-performing subs are rotated out systematically.

new_findings.title: Avoid third-party schedulers on Threads — Meta flags automation more aggressively than newer platforms

variants.claim: Meta's sensitivity to automation means third-party scheduling tools underperform natively-posted Threads content, even though they technically work.
variants.what_they_do: TDM observed anecdotally that Threads posts made via third-party tools (they call them 'outlers'/schedulers) perform worse than those posted natively. They attribute this to Meta's heightened sensitivity to automation signals. Their recommendation is to use manual/native posting for Threads, especially early in an account's life.

sources.quote: we don't recommend that you use auler. Now, it's absolutely possible and you absolutely can use them, but anecdotally, we've noticed it performed better with outulers

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses Publer for scheduling IG/social content (§5) and is expanding it in Phase 3. If Palm adds Threads to Publer's pipeline, this finding suggests native posting may outperform scheduled posting — creating a tension with Palm's automation-first approach. Telegram-routed manual posting via Amin (§5) may actually be the better fit for Threads.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Threads, route posts through Amin via Telegram (manual native posting) rather than Publer, at least during account warm-up phases. Track engagement metrics comparing native vs. Publer-scheduled Threads posts once volume allows a meaningful test. Document this as a platform-specific exception in the distribution SOP.

new_findings.title: A/B rotate creators across chat teams on 48-hour cycles to find best fit before locking in

variants.claim: Rather than committing to one chatting team immediately, rotating a new creator through multiple chat agencies on 48-hour trials surfaces the best-performing team for that creator.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter explicitly states: bounce the creator between chat teams with 48-hour rotations until finding the best one for her. This implies tracking per-team revenue performance over short windows and selecting the highest performer as the default team.

sources.quote: I would bounce the creator between chat teams with 48 hour rotations until I find the best one for her.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting to a single external team with no documented selection or evaluation process (§8 OUTSOURCED). No rotation testing, performance benchmarking, or chatter evaluation SOP exists. This is build-toward intelligence for Palm's in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the in-house chat team roadmap (§8): build a chatter evaluation framework that tests new chatters or teams on a 48-hour window against a defined revenue-per-fan metric before assigning them permanently to a creator. For the near-term with the external team: define SLA metrics (revenue/day, PPV conversion %) and review at 30-day intervals to validate the external team is optimal.

new_findings.topic: AI content sourcing criteria — motion reels preference
new_findings.title: Prioritize 'motion reels' (not static/photo) as AI inspo source for higher reach

variants.claim: Motion reels — not static image slideshows or low-movement content — consistently achieve higher reach and should be the exclusive inspo source when building an AI creator's content library.
variants.what_they_do: When scanning Instagram for AI reel inspo, the operator filters specifically for 'motion reels' (active movement, not static poses) in the 100k–1M view range. Static content is excluded from the inspo pool regardless of view count.

sources.quote: always go for motion reels and always aiming for 100 to 1 million views reels

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's §1 scrape pipeline pulls Reels broadly from inspo sources and scores them via AI vision, but does not document a motion-vs-static filter. This is an AI persona content note (§3) but the 'favor motion' heuristic is relevant to real-creator reel ideation as well given IG's algorithm preference for watchtime-driving movement.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'content type: motion vs. static' as a tagged field in the Inspiration table (§1). Filter inspo queue to surface motion reels first. For AI persona content production (§3), make 'motion reel' a hard requirement in the synthetic content SOP — only animate content with clear physical movement.

new_findings.title: Beware Telegram VA marketplaces for chatters, Reddit VAs, and traffic services — high scam rate

variants.claim: Telegram groups advertising OFM services (chatters, Reddit VAs, DM traffic, account farms) have a high rate of fraud and low-quality delivery, and agencies hiring from them under pressure will lose money and damage creator accounts.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers enumerate specific scam patterns observed: Reddit VAs promising 200 subs in 24 hours for $1k then ghosting; 'DA master' selling 10k Tinder accounts for $5k; GG (girl-for-girl) swap services delivering bot traffic from India/Brazil/Mexico rather than real Western fans; Telegram users claiming years of chatter experience who turn out to be unqualified. The root cause they identify is desperation-hiring — agencies approach these services when they're already maxed out and need someone now, which makes them easy marks for too-good-to-be-true offers.

sources.quote: beware because people on telegram are saying yeah I offer this service that service this and that da Reddit Twitter assistance and it's like you need to actually be careful with who you're approaching

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting is handled by Amin (contractor via Telegram routing, §5) and chatting is outsourced externally (§8). Palm does not document a vetting process for external service providers. Traffic/distribution strategy is a documented gap (§5). As Palm eventually builds Reddit, X, or other traffic channels and hires VAs for them, the scam vectors the speakers describe are directly applicable risks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Before Palm expands any externally-sourced traffic or VA layer (Reddit, mass-DM, GG swaps), establish a vendor vetting SOP: require trial period with small paid test before full engagement, verify deliverables with trackable links/UTMs rather than self-reported numbers, and maintain a blacklist of known bad actors. Never hire any service provider while in a capacity crunch — the speakers' 'hire slow, fire fast' principle should be a hard policy, not a suggestion.

new_findings.title: AI scores chat transcripts daily

variants.claim: An AI model trained on past chats can read every chatter's transcripts daily and surface buy-signals, missed offers, and coaching feedback that human supervisors cannot cover at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media pulls Infloww message-board exports into an LLM trained on two years of their own chats to produce daily per-chatter reports (interest/buy signals vs offers sent, left-on-read counts, missed opportunities, KPIs vs peers) because even five supervisors cannot watch 30-40 chatters live.

sources.quote: an AI LLM model that we train... based on two years worth of data of our own chats... how many interest signals... opportunities they missed

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced and Palm owns no QA loop (§8 OUTSOURCED), but AI chatting is an active Palm project. Direct build-toward for that roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's AI-chatting project, design an AI transcript-QA layer that scores every conversation daily (buy-signal capture, offers sent, misses) and feeds coaching back to chatters.

consensus_risers.title: Sell content as an escalating PPV script ladder

variants.claim: Monetize via a scripted sequence where each stage is more explicit and priced higher than the last, rather than flat pricing.
variants.what_they_do: A 'script' is pre-made content starting safe-for-work and escalating; price rises each stage, e.g. ~$10-15, then ~$35, ~$70, ~$140, ~$200. Between paid stages, send free photos at the level the fan already unlocked to over-deliver and stand out from other creators.

variants.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.

variants.claim: The first PPV sale is a warm-up purchase at a slight discount; once a fan buys it, every future PPV should be priced at the floor or higher, escalating as the conversation progresses.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers receive an automated script that pitches a first video at $34.99 (slightly below the $39.99 floor) to reduce friction and get a first purchase. After that purchase the fan is considered 'warmed up' and all future content is priced at $40+ for video and $20+ for images, escalating upward as the sexting session progresses. The escalation is tied to emotional momentum — the longer the conversation runs, the more the fan will pay.

variants.claim: Pricing the first PPV low (roughly $5-$20) exists to get the fan used to spending, not to make money on that sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard caps the first PPV at $5-$20, never higher, to get the customer used to the habit of buying on the platform before laddering up; habibi tests a brand-new buyer with a small first buy (e.g. $9) to read his boundaries.

variants.claim: The first PPV sale should always be $5 or less to condition the subscriber to buy; subsequent sales increase in price incrementally, and the overall set is structured as 7 separate sales attempts at progressively higher price points.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to structure one content 'set' as 7 distinct sales opportunities (individual pics, pic+video combos, etc.) with prices rising at each step. The first sale is always ≤$5 — not because the content is cheap, but to make the buy frictionless and establish a Pavlovian habit: sub sees PPV → sub unlocks. Subs who won't spend $5 are deprioritized or ignored. New chatters are taught to use small price increments (e.g., $5 → $7 → $10) rather than jumping to $25+. Selling too much too soon is explicitly called out as a sub-burning mistake; longevity and lifetime value are the stated goal.

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

variants.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
variants.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.

sources.quote: the first one is fairly cheap, like $10, $15, then it goes to about 35, then maybe 70, 140, and then 200.
you can send a bunch of pictures for free... it's going to seem like you're providing so much more value.
We'll start at 15 we'll then go to 25 we'll then go to 45 we'll then go to 65 and... 100 or 120
it will sell them a video for $34.99 yes it is slightly cheaper than the $40 I mentioned earlier but this is to warm them up
once they purchase this $34.99 video then you can consider them warmed up and then whenever you sell them a piece of content after that it is going to be at least 40 and upwards
price the very first pbv between $5 to $20 let's say not go any higher than that... get them used to spending
the last one nine bucks this was a first buy for him... I wanted to test his boundaries
the first sale should be under $5 should be $5 or less why because everyone can afford $5
seven separate sales so it could be one you know number one could be one pick number two could be a pick in a video

you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100
you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200 the next one for 200
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP) and is thin on OF-vault/PPV content (§2); chatting is outsourced (§8) so the sequence is build-toward for in-house/AI chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture a default PPV script ladder (stage prices + free-content-between-stages tactic) as a starting pricing position and as build-toward content for the AI-chat project.

consensus_risers.title: Run a structured onboarding call covering 6 key areas before going live

variants.claim: A thorough onboarding call is the single most important trust-building moment and should cover mindset, content expectations, timeline, invoicing, credentials, and Q&A.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a dedicated onboarding call and systematically cover: (1) model's mindset and expectations, (2) content volume needed upfront and why, (3) realistic 3-month results timeline, (4) invoicing schedule explanation, (5) credential handover for socials and OF, (6) open Q&A floor for the model. They treat the call as a formal first impression, not a casual chat.

variants.claim: A formal onboarding call presentation — covering process steps, revision limits, invoice terms, cancellation policy, and 'how to win' — eliminates scope creep and buyer remorse by setting hard expectations before any work begins.
variants.what_they_do: Build a Canva slide deck covering: welcome/team intro, reiterate buying decision with case studies, step-by-step delivery process with timelines, revision policy (2 revisions, batch feedback in one email), invoicing terms, meeting cancellation policy (72hr notice), additional services pricing, and monthly strategy call cadence. Present this at the kickoff call before any strategy discussion. Use it as a live walk-through shared on screen. Embed the deck in the client's Notion client OS for future reference.

variants.claim: A repeatable onboarding sequence — contract → account access → OF account audit → marketing setup → expectations meeting — prevents early relationship failures.
variants.what_they_do: After contract signing (via PandaDoc or DocuSign), agency logs into creator's OF account (coordinating 2FA in real time), audits and organizes the subscriber list by spend tier, organizes the content vault, sets up missing social platforms (IG, TikTok, Twitter), sends a content schedule, and holds an expectations-setting conversation covering response windows, platform norms, and month-1/2/3 milestones.

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

sources.quote: schedule that initial onboarding call and run it with the model this is that first impression
you need to establish some sort of a timeline and expectations as to when they should be able to receive the results
any frustration is just an unmet expectation. And if we're not doing a good job at setting expectations, then the client's going to be frustrated
I like to use Canva to build this out. So, if I'm on the client call... we're just going to go over a quick onboarding presentation just to make sure we're really aligned on expectations
revisions. If you do have revisions, super happy to help. We limit those to two
first step is to of course do the onboarding right so the onboarding entails a couple of different things
you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (name, email, commission %, contract, voice memo) per §6 — it is async and document-driven. There is no documented structured live onboarding call. The form captures admin data but does not walk the creator through expectations, timelines, or content strategy.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a mandatory video onboarding call as Step 2 after the contract wizard. Build a call checklist (Notion/Airtable template) covering the 6 areas above. Record it for reference. This directly addresses the creator-trust gap implied by §6's thin onboarding and supports retention (§9).

consensus_risers.title: Build a perpetual stockpile of karma-farmed Reddit accounts via VA team

variants.claim: Running Reddit at scale requires a dedicated VA farming team that continuously creates and seasons accounts to a minimum karma threshold so you always have ready inventory to rotate in when accounts get banned.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a team of VAs (plus automation) that create Reddit accounts daily, warm them for 2-3 days acting as normal users, then farm post karma (target: 2,000) and comment karma (target: 300) by posting in meme/advice subreddits. Accounts are stockpiled; when a posting account gets banned a seasoned replacement is pulled immediately from the pile. VAs are paid per account that hits the karma threshold. Automation now supplements VAs for scale.

variants.claim: Any agency new to Reddit should start with the simplest possible setup — a dedicated VA with a phone-based account posting organically to well-matched subreddits — before investing in black-hat infrastructure, because even this baseline can generate 10+ paid subs/day.
variants.what_they_do: Expert's explicit minimum recommendation regardless of agency size: assign one VA per creator for Reddit, have them use a physical phone (not desktop — Reddit deprioritises desktop activity), post organically every day or every other day to keep the account warm, no scheduling software, no automation, no upvote manipulation. Find the right subreddits through manual trial first. This single-account approach can yield 10+ paid subs/day on a well-matched creator with hundreds of viable organic NSFW subreddits available.

variants.claim: Creating Reddit accounts from scratch and warming them organically produces longer-lasting accounts than buying, because device-fingerprint mismatches on purchased accounts trigger faster bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency creates accounts fresh, runs a 3–5 day warmup (scroll, join subreddits, 1–2 upvotes/downvotes/comments per day), then begins posting. Bought accounts are acknowledged as faster but riskier. Each account gets a unique tracking/trial link, unique content (spoofed), and unique captions so no two accounts can be chain-banned via shared signals.

sources.quote: we have an account creation system every day. there's new accounts being created and there's just a farming team
An account is done seasoning if it hits 2,000 post karma and 300 common karma
get a dedicated VA. Not no scheduling, no post scheduling, nothing. If you're new to Reddit, get a dedicated VA… just posts every day in or every other day to keep the account warm
one account could definitely get you, let's say, anywhere upwards from 10 paid subs per day
we run the accounts on mobile… mobile Reddit is a lot safer and the accounts last a lot longer than they would on web
the link should be different every account… nothing should link a different account to another because that's how you can get chain banned

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit strategy at all (§5/§2 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). This is pure build-from-zero territory. Palm's Apify scraping and Publer infra exists for IG/TikTok but nothing maps to Reddit account farming or karma seasoning.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm adds Reddit to its traffic stack, do NOT start with black-hat scale. Begin with the expert's own minimum viable advice: one dedicated VA, one phone, one account per creator, organic posting to well-matched subreddits daily. Only invest in a farming pipeline after proving per-creator ROI. Document karma thresholds (2,000 post / 300 comment) as acceptance criteria for any farmed account.

consensus_risers.title: Brief chatters on model persona: tone, emojis, typing quirks, niche

variants.claim: Chatters must mirror the model's real personality and typing style (excessive letters, cute emojis, pet names) so subscribers cannot detect a VA is behind the keyboard.
variants.what_they_do: The persona section of the training PDF specifies: age range of the model → inferred typing style (e.g. 18–24 = lots of cute emojis, elongated words like 'heyyyy'), the model's content niche, any pet names she uses, and other personality markers. Chatter applies all of these verbatim.

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

variants.claim: The single highest-impact revenue intervention at $50K+/mo is an audit of chatting: verify mass messages and scripts match the creator's personality sheet, and fire chatters whose pages are underutilized — this caused a same-day revenue spike.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin and Jason logged into Barry's Inflow (chatting platform) as admins and ran a three-part audit: (1) identified what was wrong with chatting mechanics; (2) verified all mass messages and automation scripts correlated with each creator's personality sheet; (3) identified and fired unnecessary chatters — reducing from 6 to 4 — freeing the remaining chatters to work larger, more active pages. Revenue spiked the same day.

sources.quote: take note of the client's Persona so if the girl is aged between say 18 to 24 then using a lot of cute emojis when typing using a lot of excessive letters in some words

some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module
we went through all your mass messages we first identified what was wrong with your chatting and we looked at the correlation between all your personality sheets of your clients with the chatting
firing unnecessary chatters because you had like six chatters at the time when you really only needed four

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) and there is no documented persona-briefing process internally. When Palm builds in-house or AI chatting, voice consistency will be critical — especially for AI personas (§3) where breaking character destroys the illusion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'chatter voice card' field to the Creators table in Airtable (and AI Account Profile for AI personas). Populate: age, typing quirks, signature emojis, pet names, niche. Feed this card to any chatter (human or AI) at session start. Especially high priority for AI persona chatting.

consensus_risers.title: Every reel needs hook, retention, reward; 2-3/day across content pillars

variants.claim: Reels are the primary growth lever and each is engineered with a 3-second hook, mid retention and a payoff, produced at 2-3/day split across a few fixed content pillars.
variants.what_they_do: Treats reels as the top of the IG pecking order (reels > posts > stories > funnel); structures each as hook (first 3s) / retention / reward (e.g. an ugly-to-hot transformation); defines ~3 niche content pillars and split-tests them at 2-3 reels/day, doubling down on winners and cutting losers; moved from static text-on-image reels to 'clean motion' video.

variants.claim: Once a winning reel format is identified in the 10-day test, scaling to 20 trial reels per day in a specific sequence dramatically accelerates follower and subscriber growth beyond what single-daily posting can achieve.
variants.what_they_do: After the 10-day test identifies the top-performing content format, the operator switches to posting 20 reels per day using Instagram's 'trial reel' feature (which shows reels to non-followers first), in a 'specific order' the creator says he will cover in a follow-up video. This is presented as the primary scale lever once product-market fit on content is confirmed, and is credited with averaging 50+ subscribers/day.

sources.quote: Every reel should have a hook, a retention and a reward
a minimum of two quality reels per day
you're going to do the trial reel strategy which is posting 20 trial reels a day in a specific order
I average more than 50 subscribers a day with my Instagram strategies

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline scores viral reels and decides what to film (§1 MATURE) but does not codify per-reel hook/retention/reward structure or a pillar split-test cadence; real-creator cadence is a gap (§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a reel-structure standard (hook/retention/reward) and a pillar-based split-test posting cadence to the inspo pipeline output for real creators; complements Palm's existing scoring engine.

consensus_risers.topic: Organic social as primary agency scaling lever
consensus_risers.title: Scale organic social ops as the #1 agency growth lever in 2026

variants.claim: The largest OFM agencies earning hundreds of millions annually built their scale on excellent organic social content years ago, and the rest of the industry must catch up now.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant frames organic social content production — not chatting infrastructure or paid acquisition — as the defining competency separating top-tier agencies from the rest, calling for agencies to laser-focus on producing excellent creative and scaling their social media operations.

variants.claim: Content performance is governed by three levers — volume (negates luck), clarity (avatar's own language), and proof (converts attention to belief).
variants.what_they_do: Treats every post as a lottery ticket so quantity is the path to quality; insists on speaking the avatar's literal words ('tired of chatting all day') over marketing jargon; and backs claims with documented results. 'If you're not embarrassed by how much content you produce, you're not producing enough.'

sources.quote: 2026 is the year you have to get good at organic social. It is time to laser focus on producing excellent creative and scaling your social media ops
the platforms merely ask for one thing in return. Just stop making dog shit. Create content that people actually want to watch
volume negates luck... you are not in the content business, you're in the volume business
clarity beats cleverness... speak your avatar's language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already has a mature, data-driven organic social pipeline (§1) built around viral inspo scraping and an 8-step production workflow. Palm is ahead of most competitors here. However, Palm's real-creator posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is not codified (§1 GAP), which limits the ability to truly 'scale' social ops systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Palm should codify the real-creator posting cadence (frequency, platform-specific timing) to complete the §1 system. Additionally, document Palm's organic social capability as a formal competitive differentiator for use in creator acquisition pitches (§6). Consider producing case-study evidence of social growth results to use in signing conversations.

consensus_risers.title: AI personas remove model-dependency risk and keep 100% of revenue

variants.claim: Operators argue an owned synthetic creator beats managing real talent because it removes model-dependency, agency-hopping and split-revenue risk while keeping all earnings.
variants.what_they_do: Frames traditional OFM as a 'casino' (lazy models, theft, unpaid invoices, agency hoppers) where a $24k month nets only ~$6.5k after split; an AI model on Fanvue keeps 100% (minus 20% platform fee) for ~$17.5k net, fully owner-controlled and self-reliant.

variants.claim: AI creators can't use OnlyFans, so run them on an AI-friendly platform like Fanvue and prioritize realistic presentation over obvious perfection.
variants.what_they_do: OnlyFans does not allow AI; Fanvue welcomes AI, lets you label the account AI, and takes 15% vs OF's 20%. Build the persona like Build-A-Bear (face to niche, then body), batch content for both social and the paid platform, make the IG bio/handle look like a real person (uni, city, age), then funnel reels traffic to Fanvue and monetize via subscription + tips + PPV. Same can scale to ~$3-8k/mo on one model with no revenue split.

sources.quote: you get 100% share obviously with this business model
It's a casino because that's basically what it is
Only Fans does not allow AI models. It takes 20% fees... Fan View... they advocate AI creators. They take 15%.
The more realistic you make it, the better your results will be... bio that maybe says which university they're in... which city that they live in.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs real creators on OnlyFans today with revenue split (§0) and treats AI personas as a future scaling lever (§3 MATURE). Palm's AI personas still funnel to OnlyFans via social, unlike the AI-creator/Fanvue model taught here.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Hold for the future AI-creator line: validate the owner-keeps-100% Fanvue economics as the business case for a Palm AI line, separate from the real-creator OnlyFans core.

consensus_risers.title: Real models beat AI on connection, customs and platform access

variants.claim: AI personas underperform real creators because most OFM revenue comes from personal connection and custom content, and AI is pushed off OnlyFans onto less reliable platforms.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI-model courses are 'selling shovels in a gold rush', that the real money is in fans believing they're talking to a real girl and in customs AI can't do, that OnlyFans won't host AI so you're forced to Fanvue (which pays out unreliably), and that referrals from happy real creators are the best growth engine.

variants.claim: Treat an AI creator's from-scratch follower build and 'too-perfect to be real' suspicion as core risks, and prefer scaling a real creator with existing momentum.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI OFM is overhyped: content is limited (close-to-camera, props/Ferrari emblems look wrong, constant proof-checking, costs per generation), no real customs, no collabs/podcasts/in-person viral content, and you start from zero followers where a '10/10' new account looks suspicious. His own AI creator scaled to only ~$25k with huge effort; he prefers taking 50% of a real creator's fast growth over 100% of little.

sources.quote: they are all pretty much trying to sell you shovels in a gold rush
if you don't get paid, you don't get paid
you're going to be starting with an AI creator from scratch. like zero followers... how suspect it looks if your AI creator is like a 10 out of 10 chick.
we scaled a creator to $25,000. But the amount of time, energy, and effort that took. It is just not worth it compared to just working with real life creators.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Tempers Palm's AI-persona thesis (§0/§3): Palm's AI personas still funnel real social connection to OF, but this flags the customs/whale and platform-access limits and the Fanvue payout risk for any future AI-creator line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep Palm's AI personas as a social/scaling lever while concentrating customs/whale monetization on real creators; treat Fanvue-class platforms and payout reliability as explicit risks for any AI-creator expansion.

consensus_risers.title: High daily posting cadence and brand decay

variants.claim: Brand equity decays with every silent day, so consistent daily multi-post output across platforms is required maintenance, not optional.
variants.what_they_do: Posts 6x/day across IG and TikTok personally; frames missed days as compound interest in reverse where creators, algorithms, and the market all forget; only tracks posting consistency (plus inbounds, repeat viewers, proof) as a real KPI, ignoring vanity metrics.

variants.claim: Reposting low-performing content at scale wastes operational overhead with negligible subscriber return and delays the discovery of a working content formula.
variants.what_they_do: They establish a minimum quality floor first — in their case, videos that reliably hit 50K+ views each — before launching any repurposing infrastructure. Until that floor is hit, all effort goes into improving content quality (hooks, formats, etc.). Only after consistent organic performance do they add repurposing accounts and VA infrastructure.

sources.quote: I post six times a day
every time that you stop posting for a week, your brand equity decays
before you go into the volume, you have to go into the quality… optimize the content until you have a winning formula

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's real-creator posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is explicitly not codified (§1/§4 GAP); AI-account warmup cadence is fully specified (§4 MATURE).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a real-creator posting cadence (target posts/day, consistency tracking) in the SMM hub, mirroring the rigor already applied to AI-account warmup.

consensus_risers.title: Scrape a 100k-subreddit master list keyed to creator physical attributes, then niche-match posts

variants.claim: Rather than manually searching for relevant subreddits, build a scraped database of 100,000+ subreddits tagged by niche attributes so any new creator can be immediately mapped to every viable subreddit based on her physical characteristics.
variants.what_they_do: Agency built (or uses) a scraper that pulls every subreddit and tags it by niche (ethnicity, body type, fetish, etc.). When a creator is onboarded, her attributes (blonde, big breasts, e-girl, etc.) are matched against the database to generate her posting list. Volume over precision: one piece of content gets posted across every marginally relevant subreddit. Key insight: NSFW content also gets cross-posted into SFW subreddits (makeup, car repair, GeoGuessr) using curiosity/cleavage tactics to reach users who have never enabled NSFW settings.

variants.claim: Beginners should copy the structure and tone of high-performing captions in their target subreddits (not verbatim) and only develop original voice after accumulating posting experience.
variants.what_they_do: Find the most-upvoted posts in the subreddits being targeted. Extract caption patterns (length, phrasing style, call-to-action type). Write similar-but-not-identical captions. After sufficient volume, the operator develops intuition for original captions. Treat caption testing as iterative — same test-and-replace logic applied to subreddits and content.

sources.quote: what we would do is we would just have a scraper that would basically scrape every single subreddit that would or could be tied to either one of these niches. You'd build up a list of subreddits. You'd have a 100,000 subreddits

there's also a lot of say for work subreddits where people would just, you know, I don't know, ask for advice for piercings in their ears or or makeup advice. And you could also post in these subreddits
we take inspiration a lot of the time from bigger models that are doing well in the subre we post in… make it similar but not the exact same

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit presence (§5 GAP). Palm's content pipeline (§1) does use Apify scraping for IG inspiration, so the scraping capability exists — it would need to be redirected to Reddit subreddit discovery. The SFW infiltration angle is completely absent from any Palm workflow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Commission a one-time scrape of Reddit subreddits tagged by adult-content niche to build a master lookup table. Store in Airtable alongside creator attribute fields. On creator onboarding, auto-generate a subreddit posting list. Prioritise NSFW subreddits first; layer in SFW curiosity subreddits (lifestyle, hobby) only once the core strategy is proven.

consensus_risers.title: Threads 30-slave-account follow-bot: 1,200 targeted leads/day via viral-post follower targeting

variants.claim: Running 30+ Threads slave accounts that each follow 40–50 recent likers of viral similar posts generates ~1,200 targeted daily leads who see the main account funnel.
variants.what_they_do: One main Threads account posts daily content with Fanvue link. 30–50 slave accounts each have bio text 'I'm more active here' tagging the main. A custom Chrome extension bot finds recently viral posts in the niche, filters likes by recency, and auto-follows those users (40–50 per slave per session). Notified users see a profile → visit main → subscribe. AdsPower browser manages all slave accounts. The bot is distributed via private Discord.

variants.claim: A single creator can be scaled across up to 15–20 Threads accounts with near-linear compounding returns if content and operational capacity allow.
variants.what_they_do: TDM runs multiple Threads accounts per creator (up to 15–20), treating content volume and staff/hardware as the only real constraints. They use a phone farm for IG as the mechanism for managing Threads accounts and explicitly avoid automation tools (schedulers/outlers) due to Meta's sensitivity, preferring manual or native posting.

sources.quote: You need 30 50 of these. They just have to post and in the bar you can say I'm more active here and tag your main account
30 slave accounts, multiply by 40 follows, 1,200 targeted leads a day, you will get subscribers

if you've got enough content and the content performs and you see return on investment, you could run up to 15 20 accounts per creator. The bottleneck here is often times the manpower to actually run that and the hardware

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Threads strategy or follower-growth automation (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm does use Publer for scheduling but not engagement automation. This level of account farming on Threads is outside Palm's current documented ops.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona scaling line (§3), evaluate the Threads slave-account follow strategy as a traffic lever. The math (1,200 leads/day) is compelling if sustainable. Before adopting: assess Threads TOS risk (similar to IG follow-loop risks) and whether AdsPower fits alongside existing GrapheneOS/hardware isolation setup (§4). Start with a small 5-account pilot before scaling to 30+.

consensus_risers.title: Tie each team role's compensation to the specific business metric that role drives

variants.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
variants.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

sources.quote: media team are compensated for these videos doing well. Marketing team are compensated for more traffic. Chat team are compensated for more sales.
every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's team structure is thin (§10): Amin is a contractor, editors exist, and an external chat team is outsourced. No documented incentive structure or performance-linked comp bands exist. This is a GAP as Palm begins building in-house teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When hiring the first in-house chatters (§8 roadmap) and any additional editors or marketing staff, design role-specific KPIs with a variable comp component tied to those KPIs (e.g., chatters earn a bonus % of PPV revenue above a baseline; editors earn a bonus per post that hits a reach threshold). Document comp bands in the org structure build-out.

consensus_risers.topic: Creator recruiting as a permanent, dedicated department
consensus_risers.title: Treat creator recruiting as a full internal department that never stops, not an ad-hoc activity

variants.claim: Recruiting is the primary scaling lever for an OFM agency because creator quality and quantity directly determine revenue ceiling, yet most agencies treat it as an afterthought rather than a dedicated, always-on department.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs recruiting as a named department with dedicated effort and headcount, not delegated to the CEO's spare time. The principle is that the ideal creator (great look, great work ethic, fully cooperative) effectively makes the agency's systems and marketing effort secondary — and that five such creators could produce $200–300k/month. Recruiting never pauses regardless of current creator count.

variants.claim: An agency entering a new niche should develop a promotional network of at least 10–20 creators and agencies before signing any creator, so that traffic infrastructure is ready on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Before signing a single trans creator, the agency owner spends time in Telegram groups building relationships with 10–20 trans creators and several agencies that manage trans creators. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules GGs and S4S deals with this network so that as soon as a creator is onboarded, a surge of guaranteed fans (e.g. 10,000 on day one via $5K in GGs) can be deployed immediately. This inverts the usual sequence of 'sign creator, then figure out traffic.'

sources.quote: Your recruiting shouldn't stop. Your recruiting shouldn't be left on the side. It's a full department in your agency that if you don't take it seriously, you're always going to be stuck.
before you sign any trans girls, I'd say start developing your network — get at least like 10–20 trans creators that you're in contact with
as soon as you launch, have all of them pre-schedule everything so you have like $5,000 in guaranteed gains coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led with no systematic machine (§6 — THIN). The 75-conversation market research shows intentionality, but there is no documented recruiting SOP, dedicated headcount, or outbound funnel for creators. This is the agency's most documented GAP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Prioritise building a creator acquisition system as a named workstream: define an ideal creator profile (look, niche, work ethic, platform readiness), document an outbound DM sequence and qualification rubric, and assign or hire someone to run it full-time as volume scales. Track pipeline metrics (outreach sent, response rate, signed rate) in Airtable. Do not pause recruiting when the current roster feels full.

consensus_risers.title: Personal/agency brand is the real acquisition constraint

variants.claim: The binding constraint on agency growth is obscurity, not lead supply, so building a known brand is the highest-leverage acquisition asset.
variants.what_they_do: Argues all growth is constrained by irrelevance; a brand compounds trust before the first conversation so a large share of pipeline becomes inbound, and reframes competition as obscurity rather than other agencies. Notes ~7M creators means the market is nowhere near saturated.

variants.claim: The value proposition that closes creator deals in 2026 is demonstrable front-end marketing capability — organic social reach and traffic generation — not chatting or business infrastructure.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant advises that agencies pitching creators should lead with their organic social marketing system and track record of growing audiences, since that is what creators cannot do themselves and cannot easily get elsewhere at differentiated quality.

sources.quote: your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
80% of my pipeline is from inbound, from my brand

the greatest value you can possibly offer a new model is on the front end. Using organic social to create marketing campaigns and distribution networks that generate massive volumes of high quality traffic

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no agency/founder brand or content presence for acquisition (§6 THIN); every Josh DM starts from zero trust.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Have Josh (or a Palm-brand channel) publish consistent niche authority content so inbound trust precedes outreach, reducing reliance on cold DMs.

consensus_risers.title: Done beats perfect; minimize record-to-publish friction

variants.claim: Reducing friction between idea and published content matters more than production polish, because perfection kills the frequency that actually compounds.
variants.what_they_do: Films unedited YouTube videos from bullet points with just a mic and DJI Osmo + Screenflow, uses 4-5 templated thumbnails, and delegates only short-form editing; films an idea the moment it strikes rather than deferring it.

variants.claim: Batching a multi-look shoot and scheduling all resulting page posts and PPV messages in one session prevents daily scramble and ensures consistent output.
variants.what_they_do: In a single shoot session: 5 outfits/looks, each producing ~3 photos + 2 videos. Immediately after editing, schedules each outfit's photos to post on consecutive days AND schedules the corresponding PPV video message to go out on the same day as those photos. Result: 5 days of content planned in one sitting. Uses OF's native calendar scheduler.

sources.quote: perfection is what kills frequency. So done always beats perfect
I just like to reduce the friction between me having an idea and then making content

say I did five different looks today I went and did my photo shoot five different looks each look I took three photos and two videos so then what I would do is I would schedule you know tomorrow these photos the next day these photos

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's editor workflow is heavy and approval-gated (§1: 2 slots/day per creator, revisions) — appropriate for monetizable social assets but slow for founder/brand content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For any Palm/founder brand content, run a low-friction lane (bullet points, templated thumbnails, minimal editing) separate from the polished creator-asset pipeline.

consensus_risers.title: Founder should spend hours/day on the highest-leverage asset

variants.claim: Founders should reallocate large blocks of daily time to the single highest-leverage activity (brand/distribution) and treat inability to do so as a sign the business is structured wrong.
variants.what_they_do: Argues for 4+ hours/day on personal brand and says if you don't have that time, the business is set up wrong and you're stuck firefighting; pushes decentralizing operations so the founder works on leverage, not in the hamster wheel.

variants.claim: The only reliable way to test whether a $100k/month agency is truly scalable is for the founder to take a full week off and observe whether revenue, creator satisfaction, and employee efficiency hold without them.
variants.what_they_do: Liam prescribes a literal one-week founder absence as a diagnostic: if GMV and creator satisfaction hold or improve, the business is scalable; if revenue drops or creator issues go unhandled, the business is still founder-dependent and specific SOPs are missing.

sources.quote: you need to be spending 4 hours a day on this. Like minimum
if you haven't got 4 hours... a day to put into your personal brand, you're doing something wrong with your business
take a week off and just observe what happens

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin (§10): Josh carries CEO + acquisition + strategy with no org chart or delegation plan; tech/ops concentrated on Evan.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Audit founder time and protect daily blocks for the highest-leverage work (acquisition/brand), delegating firefighting — ties directly to the teammate-org initiative.

consensus_risers.topic: CRM for fan spend tracking to guide PPV pricing
consensus_risers.title: Use a fan CRM (e.g. Inflow) to read spending habits and calibrate per-fan PPV price escalation

variants.claim: A CRM that surfaces each fan's historical spend enables chatters to know exactly how far to push pricing on any individual fan rather than guessing.
variants.what_they_do: The agency uses a CRM tool called 'Inflow' that tracks per-subscriber spending history. Chatters consult this before sending a PPV to decide at what price point to pitch — fans who have spent more get higher asks. The principle is always to move price upward based on observed willingness to pay.

variants.claim: The first action inside a newly-accessed OF account should be sorting existing subscribers by spend to identify whales and avoid wasting chatter time on low-value fans.
variants.what_they_do: Upon first login to the creator's OF account, agency immediately sorts subscribers by total spend, identifying high-value fans to prioritize in DMs and deprioritizing or ignoring low-spend/no-spend subscribers.

sources.quote: if you're using a CRM a customer relationship manager like inflow then those things are going to show you the spending habits for Subs spending habits and then based on these spending habits it shows then you can only go upwards from there
organizing to subscribers seeing who's spent more and who's not organizing them so you don't waste your time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Whale Hunting tool and Fan Tracker CRM (§9, PARTIAL) that tracks fan data. However, Palm's CRM is primarily a leadership/reporting layer; there is no documented policy for chatters to use fan spend data to calibrate real-time PPV pricing. The external chat team likely does not have structured access to Palm's fan intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Bridge the §9 fan intelligence system to the external chat team: share per-fan spend summaries so chatters can price escalate based on data rather than guesswork. For the in-house chat roadmap, build a spend-aware pricing prompt directly into the chatter workflow.

consensus_risers.title: Multi-stage hiring funnel with hard filters before any offer

variants.claim: A slow, multi-stage hiring funnel with strict filtering criteria is cheaper than hiring the wrong person, whose true cost in lost time, training, and mistakes runs into tens of thousands of dollars.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a dedicated hiring funnel lasting roughly one week: job post → multiple filtering stages → skills/personality tests → pick only the single best-qualified applicant. If no applicant clears the bar, the owner does the job themselves for another 2 weeks and re-runs the funnel rather than settling. Once hired, the employee gets a fully thought-through onboarding, individual KPI tracking, and formal performance reviews.

variants.claim: Screen chatters on measurable competencies before hiring and training, since bad chatting silently destroys revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Before hiring a chatter, run an English skill test, a Wi-Fi speed test, and a words-per-minute test; only those who pass get hired and trained. Bad chatters (spelling/grammar errors, mispriced first PPVs like $4.99 or $200) are a top-7 agency-killing mistake.

sources.quote: set up a direct hiring funnel that may even take like a week to get all the applicants
we give them an English skill test... a Wi-Fi speed test... a words per minute test. Only once they check out on all of these, that is when you hire them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's hiring is not formally documented (§10 — THIN). There is no written hiring funnel, scorecard, or onboarding SOP. Josh currently drives acquisition and ops largely himself, which matches the 'doing it yourself while you find the right person' fallback, but the structured multi-stage filter does not exist.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a formal 2–3 stage hiring funnel for each role Palm needs next (e.g., editor, chatter, VA): job post → async task/test → live screen → offer. Define a written 'must-have' scorecard per role before opening any search. Document the cost-of-bad-hire math internally so the team doesn't rush to fill seats.

consensus_risers.title: Keep the profile wall teasing, never explicit, to preserve PPV value

variants.claim: Profile/wall content should tease (lingerie, not nudity) so curiosity—and willingness to pay for PPV—stays intact.
variants.what_they_do: Refuses to put nudes or explicit content on the OF wall, reasoning that if the fan sees everything for free or at subscription price there is nothing left to sell; keeps the wall at lingerie-level to sustain curiosity.

variants.claim: Pinning a priced menu of previously sent content to the OF page lets new subscribers self-serve old vault content without creator intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Creator builds a text-based menu listing past content items with descriptions and prices, pins it to their OF page. New subscribers browse it and request items by name; creator pulls from the vault folder and sends. No active selling required — the menu does the work.

sources.quote: do not put any nudes... why is the fan going to spend any money when he's basically seen all of that for free
I also created another menu that has previously sent out content so I've just organized it I label what it is and the price and then I've also pinned that to my page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on social top-of-funnel content (§1 MATURE) but thin on OF-vault/PPV content strategy (§2 THIN); this is a concrete vault-merchandising rule.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a 'tease-not-reveal on the wall' rule as part of Palm's OF-vault/PPV content guidance.

new_evidence.title: Pre-built escalating sexting scripts are the sales unit

variants.claim: Top sellers sell DM content via pre-produced, internally-consistent escalation scripts that simulate a live session and raise price as explicitness increases.
variants.what_they_do: Each 'sexting script' is a fixed 4-step bundle (SFW starter, first-tease, jugs-only, full-nude bundle) with 2-3 variations, all shot in one consistent setting/lighting; price escalates per step; ElevenLabs voice notes add realism; script content is never reused in mass-PPV or fans realize it is not live.

variants.claim: A pre-built escalation script that guides the fan through progressively more explicit content at increasing price points generates $300–$500 per subscriber per session vs. a one-off $50 custom video.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters use a fixed script structure: (1) open with small talk / lifestyle warmup, (2) send 1–2 free teaser images to raise arousal, (3) transition to selling with a tiered video ladder — e.g., $15 (tease only) → $30 (slightly more) → $60 (light action) → $120 (more explicit) → $200 (full action). Content explicitness scales with price. Subscriber buys each step sequentially; by the final video, the session is complete and they've spent $300–$500. The same script is reused across all subscribers.

variants.claim: A single well-constructed escalation script can be reused with every subscriber, making chat operations scalable without requiring chatters to freestyle or produce new custom content daily.
variants.what_they_do: The script is treated as a static, reusable asset — not a custom conversation. Chatters are trained to deliver it as if it's personal and spontaneous (the 'experience' framing), but operationally it's the same sequence for every fan. This eliminates the content-production bottleneck of true custom content (where a new video must be filmed for each request) and lets chatters handle higher fan volume with consistent outcomes.

variants.claim: A pre-made DM script that sequences plain messages, half-nude images, and full-nude PPV in a deliberate ladder is the core chatting monetization engine.
